responseSet.title: A Lacanian Perspective on Identity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Lacanian Perspective on Identity

Lionel Bailly

Lionel Bailly

London, UK
l.bailly@ucl.ac.uk

Lionel Bailly  

For most of the twentieth century the concept of identity has not been a concern for psychoanalysts, and there was an excellent reason for this. Freud’s conceptualisation of the unconscious and its dynamics had introduced a radical change in the way of thinking about the human subject, which had become deep, subtle and complex. In the words of Heinz Kohut, “identity is not a depth-psychological concept; it relates primarily to an individual’s conscious and preconscious experiences of his role and personality” (Laplanche and Kohut 1970, 177). The concept of identity cannot explain the emotional investments or the choices and the actions of an individual and fails to explain who, in truth, they are. The Freudian unconscious is a depth-psychological concept. It implies the existence of “active ideas” (Freud 1912) fuelled by the energy of the drives, of which the individual has no awareness. These unconscious ideas “are organised into fantasies or imaginary scenarios to which the instinct becomes fixated, and which may be conceived of as a true mise-en-scène of desire” (Laplanche and Pontalis 1973, 475). Some of the characteristics of the unconscious’ functioning include an absence of negation, of doubt, of degrees of certitude, an indifference to reality, and a subordination to the pleasure principle (Freud 1915). In other words, Freud tells us that human beings are largely unaware of what they really want and truly desire, and that to enjoy the objects they seek, an unconscious part of them is prepared to ignore reality, deny logic and expect satisfaction against all odds and at all costs. Thus, the speaking being utters at best half-truths, that the listener should take with caution. While in the twenty-first century this will sound disturbing to some, in a not too distant past, almost everybody knew this. Multiple examples can be found, even in popular culture. In the 1989 romantic comedy When Harry Met Sally, there is a running joke involving one of Sally’s friends, who is involved in a relationship with a married man, and who repeatedly states: “he’s never going to leave her.” Sally clearly and at times almost violently expresses the same thought, and her friend, while apparently accepting Sally’s views, continues to live as if her lover was going to leave his wife for her, and keeps repeating: “he’s never going to leave her.

responseSet.authorCitation: Azcona, M., Muller, F., Labaronnie, C., Zurita, L., Lardizábal, E. & Tolini, L.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Agreements and Differences between Psychoanalysts with Regard to Changes Observed During a Treatment. A Quantitative Exploration Using the Three-Level Model (3-LM)

Maximiliano Azcona , Felipe Muller , Celeste Labaronnie , Lic. Julia Zurita , Esp. Maite Lardizábal and Lic. Diego Tolini

Lic. Diego Tolini

La Plata, Argentina
azconamaxi@hotmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3024-8560

Buenos Aires, Argentina
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1269-9758

La Plata, Argentina
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2923-8218

La Plata, Argentina

La Plata, Argentina

Buenos Aires, Argentina

Lic. Diego Tolini  

Psychoanalysis, empirical research, Three-Level Model, clinical common ground

The aim of this paper is to report the partial results of an exploratory investigation into how twelve psychoanalysts of different theoretical-clinical orientations perceive and use hypotheses about the phenomena of change in connection with selected material from a psychoanalytic treatment. The Three-Level Model (3-LM) was used for the observation of patient transformations and for the collection of data. This was followed by the statistical analysis of the behaviour and relationship of a set of variables relating to the type and degree of change perceived in the patient's mental functioning during the course of her treatment. The results reported here show that there was significant agreement among the participants, irrespective of their theoretical-clinical orientation, as regards the following: 1) the positive impact of the application of psychoanalytic treatment in diverse areas of the patient's mental functioning; 2) the explanatory hypotheses of the changes observed in the patient under consideration; 3) the usefulness of the experience of group exchange using the 3-LM in observing and understanding the changes in the patient.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Richard Rusbridger

Richard Rusbridger

London
rjr2700@gmail.com

Richard Rusbridger  

In her paper “Meeting the Sphinx” (IJP 103: 965-978), Emma Lunbeck elaborates on a patient’s dream I had reported in my paper about a Kleinian account of the Oedipus complex (IJP 85: 731-748).

Her idea of the patient’s dream as representing both the primal scene and defences against it is consistent with what I was saying in the paper. As she says, Klein suggests that the primal scene is an exciting but terrifying object in phantasy. One solution to it, my patient’s, is to identify in an excited and omnipotent way with this object - although, in bringing her dream, she is perhaps not only living out the phantasy but asking for help in thinking about it. So patient and analyst may “both face the Sphinx” (Lunbeck, 973), but from quite different standpoints. Lunbeck describes finding meaning as being just to solve riddles (973, etc.). She seems to suggest that a stance whereby patient and analyst instead “regard [the dream] together, from a safe distance, their relationship equalized and in a way neutralized by the shared activity of watching the dream, like two moviegoers side by side in the dark” (975) is either preferable or a risk worth taking.

Lunbeck does not explain or illustrate with any clinical example what this would in practice entail. It seems to me that this dangerously overlooks just what a dangerous mental state the patient is in - full of pain and its avoidance, damaging to the patient and to her objects. This includes, in telling the dream, wishing to immobilize the analyst by enthralling him and projecting her confusion into him. The work of the analyst is not to “regard from a safe distance”, let alone, worse, in “mutual wonder” (975), as if the analyst and the patient are “equalized and neutralized”. Instead, it is to help the patient to distinguish between her phantasy and reality and so to recover or develop an adult mind. Reducing analytic work to solving riddles denudes the analyst’s function, doing to their role what my patient was, in her excited state, in part wanting to do to her analyst.

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst’s Torment: Unbearable Mental States in Countertransference, by Dhwani Shah, Oxfordshire, Phoenix Publishing House, 2023, 189 pp., $37.95, ISBN: 978-1-91-269184-5

Grace Yan

Grace Yan

yan.y.grace@gmail.com

Grace Yan  

Freud (1910) first brings countertransference to our attention as unconscious feelings arising out of the patient’s influence. Viewing it as an obstacle to the analytic process, he insists on recognizing and overcoming it through continuing and deepening self-analysis. After Heimann (1950) suggests that it is an instrument of research into the patient’s unconscious, countertransference starts to be viewed in a positive light. Especially with Bion extending the concept of projective identification, countertransference then solidifies its function in receiving the communication of unbearable emotional contents from patients. Contemporary relational theorists understand it as a joint creation by analyst and patient with varied contributions from each (Gabbard 1995). After evolving for the past century, countertransference is now widely accepted as “an unavoidable and indispensable tool in service of furthering the analytic process” (115).

With each transformation, we seem to have moved further away from Freud’s cautionary advice not to neglect the investigation of our own psyche first for the source of disturbances. Based on my own observation, there is an abundance of psychoanalytic writings discussing the use of countertransference to understand patients, but fewer on the analyst’s self-analysis. However, there exists the inherent challenge to differentiate between the patient’s projections and the analyst’s “internal reaction” (Money-Kyrle 1956).

Perhaps the analyst looking inward should not take a back seat in the exploration of countertransference. Racker (1957) states that internal appraisal “may be the greatest danger and at the same time an important tool for understanding” (303). Gabbard (1995) suggests that, for the projective identification to “stick”, there needs to be a “hook” in the recipient/analyst, which points to the necessity of analysts exploring their own intrapsychic conflicts and defences. Wilson (2013) argues that it is our ethical responsibility to examine our own conflicts before we “blame the patient”.

responseSet.title: Letter From the French Riviera
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter From the French Riviera

Johanna Velt

Johanna Velt

Nice, France
veltjohanna1@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-8894-9127

Johanna Velt  

Dear Reader,

As I write to you, the 36th Congress of the European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF), on the theme of “Illusions”, is being prepared in the wings of glamorous Cannes, known for its red carpets laid out for the world’s greatest film stars, to the delight of passers-by. But don’t let these appearances fool you! The French Riviera (or Côte d’Azur) is not all glitz and glamour … far from it! It has been the scene of terrible episodes in history, even recently.1 I would like to go through some of the milestones of the psychoanalytic movement in the South of France with you, to give you a very personal account of how challenging it can be to train and practise as a psychoanalyst in the region.

A Few Words of History

The extension of psychoanalysis into the South of France coincides historically with the expansion of the Lacanian movement and allows us to reflect on questions of distance and displacement, centralism, fiefdoms and strongholds, and the phenomena of dissidence and autonomy. Practising in the provinces is not easy because, as in many sectors of activity, Paris is central. The situation is so strong that you frequently hear mentions of “Paris and the French desert” or “internal colonialism”.

How was the extension of psychoanalysis organized? What was its policy? Was there any policy? Did psychoanalysis spread haphazardly? Or was it the convergence of well thought-through factors and other more random ones, linked to the historical, cultural or environmental specificities of the field?

Freud and Lacan shared the same concern to organize their own succession to counter the risk of the degeneration or extinction of psychoanalysis. There were two risks in particular: family lineage (Anna Freud, Jacques-Alain Miller2) and the institutional regulation of the accreditation system. In “The Question of Lay Analysis: Conversation with an Impartial Person” (1926), Freud, who was then trying to identify the most appropriate training for the analyst, wrote that it was not edicts and prohibitions that would allow psychoanalysis to develop:

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Intense Involvement in Sports, Edited by Irwin Hirsch, Philip Blumberg, and Robert I. Watson, Jr. New York, NY, USA and Abingdon, UK, Routledge, 2021, 193pp., $36.95 (paperback), ISBN 978-0-367-54238-2

Anthony Bram

Anthony Bram

Lexington, MA, USA
Anthony_Bram@hms.harvard.edu

Anthony Bram  

Psychoanalysts have a long history of interest in and writing about various sociocultural phenomena - among them, literature, visual arts, film and music. Until recently, though, applications of a psychoanalytic lens to sports have been relatively scant. This compendium of essays, Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Intense Involvement in Sports 188 edited by Hirsch, Blumberg, and Watson, reflects more recent interest in thinking psychoanalytically about athletes and sports fans (e.g. Reynoso, 2021; Rubinstein, 2017). The editors organize the book in three sections, essentially: psychoanalytic reflections on various sports-related topics, perspectives on fandom in particular, and clinical applications. Eight of the twelve chapters are reprinted from a 2010 issue of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, but readers familiar with the original articles who are interested in clinical applications will welcome this edition for the final section, in which three of four chapters are new and also vital.

The authors of two of the five chapters in the first section consider baseball from a psychoanalytic perspective. Although baseball is likely to be less familiar to, and thus possibly of less inherent interest to, some international readers, the authors explicate specific terms and historical context parenthetically or in end notes to make their explorations more accessible. The provocative title of Adrienne Harris’ chapter - “Baseball’s Bisexuality” - understates what she accomplishes in her ambitious, lyrical essay rooted in her own experiences in which her baseball fandom was associated with both (a) special connections with her father and later her husband, and (b) a sense of herself as a woman as an “envious outsider, wanting in” (26) traditionally male-dominated discourse about the sport. Harris not only integrates psychoanalytic ideas with those from feminism and postmodern gender theory, but also links them to crucial sociocultural-political critiques of sport and the rich American literary tradition of baseball writing. Her psychoanalytic perspective draws heavily on Michael Balint’s concepts including “funfare” and the object relational orientations of ocnophils versus philobats (the latter of which would make a fine name for an institute’s softball team!) to capture essential human needs met by baseball. In making her case that there is a feminine side to baseball, Harris’ writing style frequently elevates to the level of the esteemed baseball writers she admires, for example: “All the descriptions of baseball—its timelessness, its rhythmic, leisurely flow, its virtual sensuality—could easily describe or metaphorize preoedipal, pregenital, polymorphously sensual and maternal and feminine experience” (30).

[[RunningHead]]

Hating, Abhorring and Wishing to Destroy: Psychoanalytic Essays on the Contemporary Moment, Edited by Donald Moss and Lynne Zeavin, London and New York: Routledge, 2022, 262 pp., $32.55 (paperback), ISBN 978-1-032-10237-5

Tuba Tokgoz

Tuba Tokgoz

New York, NY, USA
tubatokgoz@gmail.com

Tuba Tokgoz  

My reading and reviewing of this book coincide with various tragedies in a post-COVID world, including the terror of an unjustifiable war in Ukraine and the similarly unconscionable, heartbreaking death of Mahsa Amini. Although it always is difficult to give meaning to such horrifying events, the global trauma of a pandemic, and its ensuing economic and social turmoil, further strain our efforts to comprehend. How can we understand the hatred and destruction that seem to surround us?

This timely book not only attempts to address this very question from a psychoanalytic perspective, but also claims to bring a modern perspective to psychoanalytic theorizing about the external world: in the editors’ words, “The external world matters – full stop” (7). In their introduction the editors strongly criticize the traditional psychoanalytic approach attempting to keep the “external/material reality” at bay while prioritizing the “internal/psychic reality”. They argue that conventional psychoanalysis located the source of drives and demands inside our bodies without recognizing that the external world brings its own demands and invades us like our internal world. Although the editors assert that understanding human experience as located both inside and outside informs all of the book's essays, some succeed more than others in having this dual emphasis.

In the first of three parts, entitled “Hating in the First Person Plural”, five chapters address “systematized hatreds” (15), such as racism, fascism, misogyny, homophobia and transphobia, with chapters overlapping in discussions of such issues. [[RunningHead]]

When the Fire Engulfs Us: Mind, Society, and Trauma

Carolyn Curcio

Carolyn Curcio

curcios@aol.com

Carolyn Curcio  

Chair: Stefano Bolognini

Panellists: Nancy Goodman, Douglas Chavis, Paula Ellman

Discussant: Abel Fainstein

Reporter: Carolyn Curcio

Nancy Goodman began her presentation, “Civilization and Its Discontents: The Anti-Trauma Function of Free Association When We Symbolize the Death Instinct Outside and Inside,” by alluding to the image of fires in the overall theme of the Congress. She associated them with the “wildly burning Death Instinct” of the past three-and-a-half years as expressed through the pandemic, racism, misogyny, and the invasion of Ukraine. Goodman suggested that this seemingly uncontainable barrage of events awakened terrifying unconscious fantasies and that these traumas illuminated former traumas though a Nachtraglichkeit process.

Using a series of case examples, Goodman stressed that both she and her patients have felt frightened and disoriented. She described ways in which the new analytic frame brought changes to her work with patients while stressing that fantasies continued to flourish during their sessions nevertheless. In illuminating her work with Dr D, she mentioned the fantasy of the deadly virus being situated on the computer screen. An alternative fantasy was the virus as a common enemy.

In an attempt to make sense of the upheaval, Goodman re-read Freud’s essay, “Civilization and Its Discontents.” She reiterated his theories about civilizations arising as a result of anxieties about the Death Instinct and evolving as a result of struggles between Eros and Death. In response to Freud’s hypothesis that the Death Instinct may be tamed by the formation of the superego and the subsequent presence of guilt, Goodman asked us, “What if there is no guilt?” She alluded to “psychotic rhetoric denying reality” that, at times, replaced thinking as well a fear that life forces could become overwhelmed.

Goodman described the associative process in psychoanalysis as “giving and receiving darkness and light,” and, as such, giving sessions “a flow of life.” She noted that free association can be an “anti-trauma force,” and she stressed the importance of recognizing her patients’ death wishes towards her and the importance of listening as a containing function. She mentioned becoming aware of the life force in her work as “affect is described and nightmares are told.

[[RunningHead]]

The importance of play in early childhood education: Psychoanalytic, attachment and development perspectives, edited by Marilyn Charles and Jill Bellinson. New York: Routledge, 2019, 252pp.

Reyna Cowan

Reyna Cowan

5845 College Avenue, Suite 4, Oakland, CA 94618, USA
Reynacowan@gmail.com

Reyna Cowan  

My analytic office is located on the second floor of a building with a Montessori preschool below, where the day is punctuated by the children’s outdoor playtime. Usually, I find the sound of the children’s play to be soothing and comforting. But sometimes I find myself listening in on the children and, over the years, noticing the subtle ways in which the quality of their playing has changed.

Recently, a small group of 3 ½-year-old boys were engaged in a discussion about what kind of pizza to order. As one of them held a rock up to his ear and fashioned a make-believe cell phone, the disagreement escalated, with one child insisting that his pizza needed to be both vegan and gluten-free. A second boy squatted on the ground and insisted that he was trying to be flexible, but vehemently protested that “a vegan pizza was not a pizza”. As the argument became quite heated, the third boy’s eyes darted back and forth, from one of his two playmates to the other, with the expression of someone tasked with solving this dilemma. Suddenly, he grabbed the “rock telephone” and threw it far away from his playmates.

Unfortunately, it happened to be a moment when there were no preschool teachers nearby. It would have been intriguing to observe what approach the teacher would have used to intervene if they would even choose to intervene at all. And soon I found myself flooded with a cascade of questions: What would the teachers notice? Would they see this interaction as a moment of engaged play, or by contrast, a moment of failed play? What would they think was being enacted? Were the teachers familiar with the parents and families of these three children? And most importantly, how would I, as a child clinician, think about intervening with these children, or talking with the preschool teachers, about what was being communicated?

Shortly after this experience, I was excited to receive the compendium The Importance of Play in Early Childhood Education: Psychoanalytic, Attachment and Development Perspectives, a book created for early childhood educators with the goal of generating new ways of thinking about play in the preschool setting. In particular, this collection of essays introduces preschool teachers to psychoanalytic thinking about play in early childhood education. Edited by Marilyn Charles and Jill Bellinson, this volume draws from the work of developmental psychologists, education specialists and psychoanalysts to reveal how these fields capture ideas about play in relation to preschoolers. Each chapter is written from the author’s specific angle about issues such as understanding play, play in the classroom, techniques of play, etc., with the specific goal of creating an overview of how to think about play.

Gender Dysphoria: A Therapeutic Model for Working with Children, Adolescents and Young Adults, by Susan Evans and Marcus Evans, Bicester, Phoenix Publishing House, 2021, £27.99, ISBN: 13: 978-1-912691-78-4

Alan Colam

Alan Colam

Alan.Colam@candi.nhs.uk

Alan Colam  

“Gender dysphoria” is described in the fifth edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) of the American Psychiatric Association as a condition in which a person experiences psychological discomfort or distress because there is an incongruence between their biological sex and their gender identity (the individual’s psychological sense of gender). These feelings may arise in early childhood (as defined by the 10th and 11th revisions of the World Health Organization’s International Classification of Diseases [ICD-10 and ICD-11]), but in the broader terms of DSM-5, may appear at any age.

Recent years have seen an escalation in the presentation of what has become known as rapid-onset gender dysphoria, particularly in adolescents and especially, in the UK, among young biological females who wish to transition to male. This has taken place in a context in which the “affirmation model” has tended to dominate, so that gender dysphoria has led to the assumption that the individual expressing this distress is transgender. Consequently, if they so desire, they should transition to the sex reflecting their gender identity. That gender dysphoria necessarily implies transgender and should lead to transition is the affirmative assumption that this book seeks to question.

Both authors worked for many years at the Tavistock Centre, a psychoanalytic treatment and education centre in London that houses several clinics of which the Gender Identity Disorder Service was one. Referrals to this service increased from approximately 100 in 2005 to 2500 within a decade, the demographic changing from 66% males wishing to transition to female to 66% females wishing to transition to male. Serious concerns about this service were raised as early as 2005 but became more urgent from about 2018, when concerns were raised by some parents and staff about the treatment of minors presenting with gender dysphoria. Their particular concern focused on the use of medicalized treatments potentially leading to medicalized transition, without sufficient psychological assessment and exploration. The concerns raised about this led in the UK to a judicial review, which in 2020 ruled that a child under the age of 16 may only consent to the use of medication intended to suppress puberty where he or she is competent to understand the nature of the treatment. responseSet.title: The Use of Elements of Peirce's Philosophy by Four Well-Known Psychoanalytic Authors
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Use of Elements of Peirce's Philosophy by Four Well-Known Psychoanalytic Authors

Paulo Duarte Filho

Paulo Duarte Filho

Rua dos Tamanás 358, Vila Madalena, São Paulo - S.P., Brazil - CEP 05444-010
pduartegf@uol.com.br

Paulo Duarte Filho  

Peirce's philosophy, psychoanalytic authors, interdisciplinary writings

This article highlights elements of Peirce’s philosophy used by four well-known psychoanalytic authors, Ricardo Steiner, André Green, Bjorn Salomonsson and Dominique Scarfone, showing how contributions from Peirce’s ideas could clarify psychoanalytic matters. The subject of Steiner's paper is how Peirce’s semiotic could help to fulfill a conceptual gap mainly in Kleinian tradition in relation to phenomena that occur between what are called “symbolic equations” (representations lived as facts, by psychotic patients) and symbolization. Green's writings question Lacan’s conception that the unconscious is structured like a language, suggesting that Peirce’s signs, particularly the icons and indices, would be more appropriate to think about the unconscious than the linguistics used by Lacan. One of the Salomonsson's papers gives a good example of how Peirce’s philosophic notions can be enlightening in the clinical area, as they are used to answer the criticism that words could not be understood by a baby in a “mother-infant” treatment; the other uses Peirce's conceptions to give interesting suggestions about Bion's beta-elements. The last paper, from Scarfone, broadly address the constitution of significations in psychoanalysis, but we will limit ourselves to consider how Peirce's concepts are used in the model proposed by Scarfone.

[[RunningHead]]

Opera on the Couch: Music, Emotional Life, and Unconscious Aspects of Music, edited by Steven H. Goldberg and Lee Rather, Abingdon & New York, Routledge, 2022, 224pp., $39.95, ISBN: 9781032210773

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

a.sabbadini@gmail.com

Andrea Sabbadini  

It is not surprising to find frequent references in the literature to the relationship between music and psychoanalysis, given that our emotional life is so closely associated from our earliest days with musical experiences, Freud's self-confessed indifference to music notwithstanding. Numerous articles are published about it, although books are sparse and are mostly edited collections of papers (e.g. Feder, Karmel and Pollock 1991 & 1993), with only a handful of exceptions (e.g. Nagel 2013; Kennedy 2020).

The multiple ways in which psychoanalysis intersects and interacts with music are undoubtedly fascinating, but they also remain difficult to grasp. These texts either suggest a reading of specific compositions from a psychoanalytic perspective by identifying a parallel between their formal structure and psychodynamic models of mental functioning; or they explore more generic theoretical aspects, such as the neuropsychological impact of music on its listeners; or attempt to attribute the significance of certain musical works to the personality or psychopathology of their composers. Classical compositions that contain a verbal text (such as operas, lieder or choral oratorios) are less abstract than purely musical works (such as symphonies, concertos or string quartets) and are the most common subjects of these psychoanalytic studies. Yet, as far as I know, there has never been a psychoanalytic book published in English that is exclusively focused on opera. The volume under review here, Opera on the Couch: Music, Emotional Life, and Unconscious Aspects of Mind, is then a welcome first of its kind. It originated from, and was inspired by, a programme of events at the San Francisco Opera where Sunday matinée performances were followed by presentations and discussions with the audience - a programme (also named Opera on the Couch) in which psychoanalysts Steven H. Goldberg and Lee Rather, the two book editors, were actively involved.

In their comprehensive introductory chapter on the “felicitous match” between psychoanalysis and opera, Goldberg and Rather suggest that the synergistic form of melodrama is well suited to psychoanalytic investigations because of its “potential to convey emotional complexity and conflict on multiple levels and in multiple registers simultaneously” (1) and because opera displays in words and music such universal themes as love, death, power and faith, which are also frequently heard from the couch. These authors’ conclusion is that opera is a privileged art form for interdisciplinary studies. The exploration of the relationship between psychoanalysis and opera then allows us to gain a fresh understanding of those artistic works under scrutiny and, given the richness and complexities of the operatic universe (its stories, characters and music), may also provide us with new perspectives on our analysands’ unconscious desires, conflicts and motivations.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Proof: Revisiting Freud’s Tally Argument
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Proof: Revisiting Freud’s Tally Argument

Michael Michael

Michael Michael

Seoul, Republic of Korea
mmichael.esq@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-6259-6450

Michael Michael  

Epistemology, Adolf Grünbaum, Inference to the Best Explanation, Insight, Holism

This paper explores the notion of proof in clinical psychoanalysis by reconsidering an argument Freud made concerning the relation between successful psychoanalytic treatment and truth, dubbed the “Tally Argument” by the philosopher Adolf Grünbaum. I first reiterate criticisms of Grünbaum’s reconstruction of this argument, which bring out the degree to which he has misunderstood Freud. I then offer my own interpretation of the argument and the reasoning that underlies its key premise. Drawing from this discussion, I explore three forms of proof, each inspired also by analogies with other disciplines. Laurence Perrine’s “The Nature of Proof in the Interpretation of Poetry” stimulates my discussion of inferential proof, the relevant form of which involves proving an interpretation through a strong enough Inference to the Best Explanation. Mathematical proof stimulates my discussion of apodictic proof, of which psychoanalytic insight is a fitting example. Finally, holism in legal reasoning stimulates my discussion of holistic proof, which provides a reliable means by which therapeutic success can verify epistemic conclusions. These three forms of proof can play a crucial role in ascertaining psychoanalytic truth.

Psychoanalysis Meets Psychosis. Attachment, Separation, and the Undifferentiated Mind, by Michael Robbins, 2019, New York, London, Routledge, Pp. 206., 34,99£, ISBN 9780367191177

Riccardo Lombardi

Riccardo Lombardi

dr.riccardolombardi@libero.it

Riccardo Lombardi  

Michael Robbins is the author of several important books including Experiences of Schizophrenia: An Integration of the Personal, Scientific, and Therapeutic (1993) and The Primordial Mind in Health and Illness. A Cross-Cultural Perspective (2011), which explore the world of schizophrenia, its implications in relation to the functioning of the primordial mind, and its connection to the sociocultural. Unfortunately, his works have never been reviewed for the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, making his most recent book Psychoanalysis meets Psychosis. Attachment, Separation, and the Undifferentiated Mind a timely opportunity for appreciating his work. The author commands a vast history of clinical experience, research and teaching regarding psychosis and primitive mental states. He writes:

Socio-cultural alienation is rooted in widespread unconscious acceptance of the biases of the dehumanizing organic medical model on the one hand, and those of a psychoanalysis that looks upon psychotic persons as constitutionally unable to become normal or neurotic on the other. As a result, psychoanalysis has rejected psychosis as beyond its scope and abandoned it to medical society, that conceives of it in a way that alienates the subject from his or her mind. This social alienation has been rationalized as scientific. … Schizophrenia, incidentally, is an unfortunate label because the social stigma attached promotes alienation. (174-176)

These affirmations neatly capture Robbins’ scientific excellence where he challenges the discriminating labels of pathology and health in promoting an integral vision of the human being. It is only the dominance of one aspect over the other that determines the external 793

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Identity in vivo: Permanence and Change
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Identity in vivo: Permanence and Change

Ricardo Bernardi

Ricardo Bernardi

Montevideo, Uruguay
ric.e.bernardi@gmail.com

Ricardo Bernardi  

Psychoanalytic Identity, clinical discussion groups, Three-Level-Model

Psychoanalytic identity “in vivo” means psychoanalysis as an authentic, lived experience, socially and historically situated. What we have inherited from the psychoanalytic tradition now needs to be collectively and individually updated and shaped as therapy, as research and as theory. The focus of this paper is on developing a more critical and realistic sense of psychoanalytic identity, grounded in our clinical experience. We need to recognise our identity in what we actually do and achieve with our patients in our daily practice and avoid idealisations or devaluations arising from theoretical speculation. The important role of the Three Level Model (3-LM) and similar working parties is discussed. Psychoanalysts need a pluralistic professional identity, which implies triangulating our clinical perspectives with those of other colleagues, as happens in 3-LM clinical discussion groups, and contextualizing our knowledge from a broad perspective, including extra-clinical research and interdisciplinary dialogue with both health sciences and hermeneutic disciplines. A psychoanalytic identity that is open to the future requires an acknowledgement of the different positions that exist within our discipline and neighbouring fields, and a willingness to critically examine and discuss these differences.

Jacques Lacan

Jacques Lacan

Jacques Lacan  

The development of Freud's views on the ego led him to two apparently contradictory formulations. The ego takes sides against the object in the theory of narcissism: the concept of libidinal economy. The bestowal of the libidinal cathexis on one's own body leads to the pain of hypochondriasis, while the loss of the object leads to a depressive tension which may even culminate in suicide.

On the other hand, the ego takes sides with the object in the topographic theory of the functioning of the perception-consciousness system and resists the id, i.e. the combination of drives governed solely by the pleasure principle. If there be a contradiction here, it disappears when we free ourselves from a naive conception of the reality principle and take note of the fact—though Freud may have been clear on this point, his statements sometimes were not—that while reality precedes thought, it takes different forms according to the way the subject deals with it.

Analytic experience gives this truth a special force for us and shows it as being free from all trace of idealism, for we can specify concretely the oral, anal, and genital relationships which the subject establishes with the outer world at the libidinal level. I refer here to a formulation in language by the subject, which has nothing to do with romantically intuitive or vitalistic moods of contact with reality, of his interactions with his environment as they are determined by each of the orifices of his body. The whole psycho-analytic theory of instinctual drives stands or falls by this.

What relation does the “libidinal subject”, whose relationships to reality are in the form of an opposition between an Innenwelt and an Umwelt, have to the ego? To discover this, we must start from the fact—too often neglected—that verbal communication is the instrument of psycho-analysis. Freud did not forget this when he insisted that repressed material such as memories and ideas which, by definition, can return from repression, must, at the time when the events in question took place, have existed in a form in which there was at least the possibility of its being verbalized. By dint of recognizing a little more clearly the supra-individual function of language, we can distinguish in reality the new developments that are actualized by language. Language has, if you care to put it like that, a sort of retrospective effect in determining what is ultimately decided to be real. Once this is understood, some of the criticisms which have been brought against the legitimacy of Melanie Klein's encroachments into the pre-verbal areas of the unconscious will be seen to fall to the ground.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Travelling through Time. How Trauma Plays Itself Out in Families, Organizations and Society, by M. Gerard Fromm, Oxfordshire, Phoenix Publishing House, 2022, £26.09, ISBN-13:978-180013-025-8.

Sverre Varvin

Sverre Varvin

svvarv@oslomet.no

Sverre Varvin  

“If families resemble trees, as they say, arborescent structures with entangled roots and individual branches jutting out at awkward angles, family traumas are like thick translucent resin dripping from a cut in the bark. They trickle down generations” (Shafak 2021, 128).

This remarkable book by M. Gerard Fromm is a deeply moving story about how “cuts in the bark” of families and societies make traumatic “resin trickle down generations”. Shafak’s story tells how the violent civil war between the Turks and Greeks in Cyprus had an effect as the “cut in the bark” – the traumatic destruction of the protective shield – and led to the encapsulation of wounds transmitted to the following generations. Fromm refers to Davoine and Gaudillier’s work on how traumas have hidden histories, a traumatic heritage from earlier generations (Davoine and Gaudilliere 2004), where:

one task of transmission turns out to be the generational struggle to resist the dissociation of that heritage, to recognize, in Davoine and Gaudilliere’s phrase, the cut out unconscious (Davoine and Gaudilliere, 2004, p. 61). The goal is to bring the cut out back into the official narrative, whether the narrative is the family’s or the society’s – and to bring its full tragic story into social discourse. (Fromm, 2022, 96)

Unresolved traumatization leaves a heavy burden on the coming generations, but also the possibility of relief for families and groups when the descendants have the possibility to work through unfinished mourning.

Trauma becomes trauma because it is suffered alone. This is a simple, profound definition of why dire experiences become traumatic for many people. The traumatized person needs another, a witness, who then can become a third, who not only can help put words to and decipher the “cut-out” story, but also give some meaning to experiences that for most of us are beyond any understanding, like torture. Fromm’s book is a witness for the many who have suffered atrocities, but it also represents a third voice helping to bring meaning to experiences. This helps the trauma survivor to be more present, to put their life in order and, as Winnicott holds, to experience the trauma as a subject. A trauma of the past has not really happened, because the subject was not there for it to happen (Winnicott 1974). The witness, in a third position, needs to be there, and only then can the difficult work of mourning start. I think it is necessary to be cautious here because traumatization will set inedible marks in the soul and in the family’s roots, ones that are often impossible to integrate. Not being alone with pain is, nevertheless, of immense importance.

responseSet.title: Passivity as a Defence and Disguised Destructiveness
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Passivity as a Defence and Disguised Destructiveness

Ursula Ostendorf

Ursula Ostendorf

Hamburg, Germany
u.ostendorf@web.de

Ursula Ostendorf  

Defence, masochism, passivity

In this paper, I set out to describe the different viewpoints, conceptualisations and defence mechanisms of the state of passivity; the categorisation by Freud; how the perspective of his thinking was altered by later insights and clinical observations; the close connection between the superego, passivity and masochism; the significance of the internal object world for Melanie Klein; countertransference as a means of access to masochism and destructiveness, with the aid of a short case illustration; and, finally, Betty Joseph's clinical experiences in work with her patients.

responseSet.title: Remembering, Repeating and Working-Through as a Step in Freud’s Ongoing Struggle with the “What”, “Why” and “How” of Analytic Knowing in the Curative Process
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Remembering, Repeating and Working-Through as a Step in Freud’s Ongoing Struggle with the “What”, “Why” and “How” of Analytic Knowing in the Curative Process

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

13 Gikhon St, Jerusalem 9354713, Israel
Rachelblass@gmail.com

Rachel Blass  

Freud, Klein, Papers on technique, repetition compulsion, working-through, epistemophilia

In this paper the author offers a new reading of Freud's “Remembering, Repeating and Working-through”, examining the complex nature of central concepts that Freud presents within it. She demonstrates the text's special role in an ongoing effort of Freud's to articulate and ground the heart of his analytic insight that knowledge cures. While the insight itself is very well-known, the fact that Freud struggled throughout his life with its articulation and grounding is not. The struggle centered on questions pertaining to how analytic knowing could, not only enlighten the patient, but actually change his unconscious dynamics, and why the patient, having already “opted” for pathology in place of knowing would come to accept it; and ultimately, what was the nature of the knowledge offered in analysis and the individual's relationship to it that allowed for such dramatic changes to occur. The author briefly presents some of her earlier work on Freud's struggle with these issues and how Melanie Klein resolved them. It is in this context that she demonstrates how in Remembering, Repeating and Working-through” Freud may be seen to be taking important steps towards developing his ideas on analytic knowing and in ways that anticipate Klein's resolutions. This points to the close tie between Klein's and Freud's thinking on the nature of the analytic process and the person's desire for self-knowledge on which it relies, brings out the richness of this thinking and grounds its value to contemporary psychoanalysis.

Andrea Quiroga , Santiago Juan , Sherwood Waldrom , Laura Bongiardino , Saskia Aufenacker , Alan Crawley , Camila Botero and Laura Borensztein

Laura Borensztein  

3-LM, trustworthiness, CQR, OPD-2, qualitative analysis, training

The 3-Level Model (3-LM) is proposed as a guide or heuristic for observing and describing patient change. Used since 2011 in the context of the International Psychoanalytic Association (IPA), its trustworthiness as a model still needs to be studied. OBJECTIVES AND METHODS: This study aimed to assess the trustworthiness of the 3-LM by comparing the output of three groups of IPA-certified analysts (Europe, North America and South America). The comparison was made using process and outcome measures as analytical tools. This objective was divided into specific objectives presented in two articles. Each group belonged to a different geographical region of the IPA. They all worked on the same clinical case and their output was analysed using the same structured qualitative methodology. To analyse levels 1 and 3 of the model, the Consensual Qualitative Research (CQR) method was used. For level 2, the Operationalised Psychodynamic Diagnosis (OPD-2) was used. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION: There was a predominance of convergence between each of the groups for each of the levels analysed, with some points of divergence. The implications of these results for the trustworthiness of the model, clinical practice, training and research in psychoanalysis are discussed.

responseSet.title: Work Discussion for Community Mental Health
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Work Discussion for Community Mental Health

Ricardo Readi

Ricardo Readi

Cassel Hospital, London, UK
rreadig@gmail.com

Ricardo Readi  

Bion, menzies, institutional dynamics, work discussion, social defense system

This paper aims to emphasize the pertinence of psychoanalytic work in the context of communitary mental health. The theoretical orientations for this are based on the concept of Social Defence Systems, as introduced by Jaques and developed by Menzies, and the intervention method used is Work Discussion, an original and applicable approach designed and consolidated at the Tavistock Clinic. With this contributions, we are able to consider the ways in which Institution´s malfunctioning relates to ways in which it´s work implies a defensive activity with which its participants, workers and patients, can be unconsciously colluded. After describing this method and the mentality behind it, this work describes in detail its application in the context of a Communitary Mental Health Centre in Santiago, Chile. Some clinical examples are included, together with some thoughts about the value that this intervention has for the community.

[[RunningHead]]

Raw Reveries, Polaroid Reveries: Some Hypotheses About Possible Functions and Technical Uses of Reveries in the Session**

Violet Pietrantonio

Violet Pietrantonio

Violet Pietrantonio Via Donzelle, 1 Bologna Italy
violetpietrantonio@gmail.com

Violet Pietrantonio  

Reverie, transformation in dreaming, unrepresantable state of mind

The author tries to show how the epiphany of a reverie during a session can become a source of unexpected intuitions about the essence and possible figurability of the emotional experience which is flowing in the here-and-now of the analytic life. Reverie becomes an important analytic source above all when an analyst is in contact with primordial states of the mind, characterized by turbulences of unrepresentable feelings and sensations. In this paper the author outlines a hypothetical kit of functions, technical uses and analytic effects of a reverie in an analytic process, working through the idea of analysis as the transformation in dreaming of the nightmares and terrors which beset and trouble the patient’s mind. In particular the author describes: (a) the use of reverie as a criterion of analysability in first consultation meetings; (b) the specificity of two different types of reverie, which the author names reveries polaroid and raw reveries; and (c) the possible disclosure of a reverie in case of a specific kind of reverie that the author calls polaroid reverie. Sketches of analytic life become living portraits of the hypothesis proposed by the author about these different possible uses of a reverie in analytic work, as a probe and resource for beginning and developing a course of analysis that engages with archaic and presymbolic areas of psychic functioning.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and the Third Position: Social Upheavals and Atrocity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Third Position: Social Upheavals and Atrocity

Sverre Varvin MD Dr. Philos

Sverre Varvin

Oslo, Norway

Gustav Vigelands vei 40 0274, Oslo, Norway
svvarv@oslomet.no

Sverre Varvin  

Many situations are now characterized by a breakdown of order and structure, leaving people at the peril of unorganized forces (war machines, human traffickers, etc.) resulting in the dehumanizing of ordinary people on a mass scale, especially in the refugee field. The paper focuses on how alienating discourses on “trauma” and society’s neglect of traumatized people increase suffering and have grave consequences for coming generations. It reflects on how psychoanalysis may represent a mediating function in relation to regressive processes at individual, group and societal levels. A conceptualization of a third position from which psychoanalysis can work is developed. The third position is seen as inevitable in psychoanalytic clinical work in that symbolization and working though must be anchored in a common cultural discourse. A model for rethinking traumatization is proposed that develops the conception of the third position in relation to a broader field and encompasses the subject’s relations to dyadic, bodily-affective relations, to the group and family, and to culture/ discourse. This model may lay the groundwork for understanding how atrocities and social catastrophes such as collective traumatization can be worked through at the individual and social levels. Clinical examples are presented to illuminate these processes.

responseSet.title: Receptivity is Not Passivity: A Comparison between Psychoanalysis and Phenomenology Concerning Experience, Judgement and the Analytic Attitude
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Receptivity is Not Passivity: A Comparison between Psychoanalysis and Phenomenology Concerning Experience, Judgement and the Analytic Attitude11

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Myliusstrasse 20, D-60323 Frankfurt a.M., Germany
heinzweiss3@outlook.de
weiss@sigmund-freud-institut.de

Heinz Weiss  

Passivity, receptivity, phenomenology, analytic attitude

Starting from Edmund Husserl’s last book Experience and Judgement, this paper explores the notions of “passivity” and “receptivity” in phenomenology and psychoanalysis. Both sciences agree that receptivity differs from passivity, but they have developed different conceptualizations about the very nature of the rudimentary “ego-activity” which is the source of receptivity. In phenomenological terms, “pre-predicative” experience roots in a primary presence and openness of the ego towards the world, whilst psychoanalysis has emphasized the role of projective and introjective processes which are close to bodily experiences and unconscious phantasy. The second part of the paper draws some conclusions concerning the analytic situation, in particular the shift among receptivity, empathy, curiosity and creative imagination as central features of the analytic attitude from a mainly Kleinian point of view. The paper argues that receptivity is a field where phenomenological and psychoanalytic approaches can mutually enrich and learn from each other.

responseSet.title: On the Analytic Transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Analytic Transference

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

France
dominique@bourdin.biz

Dominique Bourdin  

Transference, countertransference, setting

This paper is a presentation of the concept “transference” in psycho-analysis. In Freud's thought, transference movements are new editions, copies of tendencies or phantasies that are the repetition of infantile prototypes, relived with a lively feeling of actuality. The text shows Freud's reflection on transference, and the importance of the countertransference which has been much developed by the post-freudians authors. Then, the principal forms of transference are presented, for example passionate transference, or transference by reversal. The psychoanalytic reflection about transference leads to study the analyst's neutrality, and to pay attention to the setting of the sessions. André Green emphasizes that the transference that is addressed to the analyst is at the same time a transference on the setting and on to speech. In the complexity of the “analytic situation” (Donnet), speech is transformed under the effect of the transference address (Rolland).

responseSet.title: Lacan and the Transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lacan and the Transference

Gilbert Diatkine

Gilbert Diatkine

Paris, France
gilbert.diatkine@wanadoo.fr

Gilbert Diatkine  

Signifier, après-coup, subject division

Lacan papers reading is difficult and often disappointing. His technique, using shortening of sessions as a way of interpreting transference (“scansion”) is unacceptable. His great project of create a united structural theory of psychoanalysis, linguistic and anthropology has failed. However, his works on transference are worth being red. Lacan has reminded psychoanalysts that they are “divided subjects”, meaning that their unconscious remains unconscious to themselves, that they have to listen to themselves as much as they have to listen to their their patients; he has rediscovered the importance of après-coup, already stressed by Freud, but often forgotten; and above all, he has rediscovered the importance of words in transference interpretation, showing the importance of the link between two signifiers, more than the link between signifier and signified.

Debates in Psychoanalysis: A Latin American Perspective

Luisa Suquilvide

Luisa Suquilvide

Montevideo, Uruguay

Luisa Suquilvide  

Pluralism, Controversies, Evidence, Three-Level Model

While pluralism allows for the recognition of different psychoanalytic models, it still remains a challenge to build processes and arguments strong enough to resolve basic disagreements. The Three-Level Model constitutes a valuable tool to an open critical discussion among analysts of different theoretical orientations regarding a clinical material, by providing sources of evidence about patient transformations in an analytic process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis My Way, by Anders Zachrisson, Oslo, Kolofon Forlag, 2021, 471 pp., NOK 299, ISBN 978-82-300-2227-6

Eike Hinze

Eike Hinze

e.f.hinze@t-online.de

Eike Hinze  

The Norwegian analyst Anders Zachrisson starts his introduction with the following sentences:

My journey is approaching the point that Shakespeare described in his Seven ages of man:

“ … The last scene of all, that ends this strange eventful history, Is second childishness and mere oblivion, sans teeth, sans eyes, sans taste, sans everything.” (1)

Facing this inevitable reality, I have surrendered to a wish to round off my professional life by publishing a selection of my papers from the last couple of decades. I do it for my friends, for myself, and to handle the flight of time.

This introduction bears resemblance to Frank Sinatra’s famous song from 1969 “I Did it my Way”, which is alluded to in the book’s title: “Psychoanalysis my Way”. This song starts with the lines:

And now the end is near

And so I face the final curtain

My friend, I’ll say it clear

I’ll state my case of which I’m certain.

This book is the fruit of a whole analyst’s lifetime. At first sight one may shrink back from this selection of papers of a single analyst, published during the last 22 years. A question may cross one’s mind: is this another collection of papers which may be of interest only to a local institute? Reading the book, however, quickly dispels such apprehensions. This book does not just consist of a loose collection of papers. On the contrary, it demonstrates how an analyst deals in depth with his professional life and his lifelong professional development. It resembles in an existential way Frank Sinatra’s famous song in which the singer deals with his life. It is this existential dimension that makes the book so fascinating. The picture on the book cover shows vaults connected in an Escher-like fashion and a motto at the beginning of the book very aptly serves as an introduction:

Steps, vaults and gates; dead ends and forking passages; bewildering richness – human mind.

[[RunningHead]]

Three Characters: Narcissist, Borderline, Manic Depressive, by Christopher Bollas, Bicester, Oxon, Phoenix Publishing House, 2021, $23.95, 76pp., ISBN 978-1-912691-81-4

Nancy de Holl

Nancy de Holl

New York
nancydeholl@gmail.com

Nancy de Holl  

In his latest book, Three Characters, Christopher Bollas animates a trifecta of psychoanalytic characters: the Narcissist, the Borderline and the Manic Depressive. The essays are derived from lectures by Bollas for a series of seminars in Chicago and Sweden spanning nearly thirty years. A unique reading experience in clinical writing, the book illuminates the psychic reality of character pathology through the convention of the character sketch.

Implementing an exercise from his literary studies, the author projects himself into each character type so that we may “get into the minds” of these personalities and appreciate the intelligence of their defensive structures. In doing so he orients the reader toward the transient depersonalization one may have while reading a work of literature or while listening to an analysand if we are successful enough in putting our objectifying impulses aside. In some psychoanalytic corners, these are entities one may hear described in terms of identifiable characteristics, types of cognition, modes of functioning and so on (consider Shapiro’s Neurotic Styles as an ego-psychological point of contrast). Here Bollas renders the “psychodynamic and object-relational logic” of character pathology in a spacious, gestural manner that at times called to mind humoral cosmology or Dürer engravings: theories of personality types that pre-date rationalist science.

In these pages, one can feel how Bollas has spent decades venturing to enter states of deep identification with his analysands. Drawing from his own idiomatic repository of clinical 404

responseSet.authorMast: José A. Castillo-Garayoa and  Ramón Echevarría
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on the Reception of Attachment Theory by Psychoanalysts. A Review of Publications

José Castillo-Garayoa and Ramón Echevarría

Ramón Echevarría

Barcelona, Spain
josepantonicg@blanquerna.url.edu
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-9421-6643

Barcelona, Spain

Barcelona, Spain

Ramón Echevarría  

Psychoanalysis, attachment, pluralism in psychoanalysis, historical research

This article explores the reception and response of the psychoanalytic community to attachment theory by reviewing articles, with 'attachment' as a keyword, published up until December 2020 in the top seven (according to impact factor) English-language psychoanalysis journals in the ISI Web of Knowledge. A category system was designed and applied to classify the articles which met the inclusion criteria.

Since 1996, there has been a significant increase in publications that compare attachment theory and psychoanalysis, explore avenues of integration, analyse different theoretical and technical aspects, or make eclectic use of concepts from both models. Overall, however, few articles referring to attachment (246, representing 1.8% of the sample) have been published in the analysed journals, and of those, many hardly make any mention of psychoanalysis.

Contributions by the attachment theory pioneer, John Bowlby, emerged from psychoanalytic practice, with attachment theory reinforcing the more relational approaches of psychoanalysis. Nonetheless, a main conclusion of our study is that attachment and psychoanalysis follow parallel or divergent paths.

responseSet.title: What Constitutes a Psychoanalytic Identity?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Constitutes a Psychoanalytic Identity?

Elizabeth Allison

Elizabeth Allison

London, UK
e.allison@ucl.ac.uk

Elizabeth Allison  

The concept of identity seems only latterly to have become a concern for psychoanalysts. Psychoanalysis arguably has provided the most subtle and nuanced account available of the constitution and continuous evolution of identity through processes of projection, identification, introjection, repression and sublimation: identity is not given, nor is it fixed and stable, and it is always riven with contradictions due to the mind’s dynamic nature. And yet we are all very familiar with the wish to make definitive statements about psychoanalytic identity: to say with certainty what is or is not psychoanalysis, and this wish goes all the way back to Freud, who in 1914 proposed that “There should be some headquarters whose business it would be to declare: 'All this nonsense is nothing to do with analysis; this is not psycho-analysis'” (Freud 1914, 23). This “headquarters” was of course the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA). So this need has been with us from the beginning, but evidence suggests that it has been felt to be more pressing in recent years (e.g. Blass 2010; Bohleber 2010; Busch 2023; Foresti 2018; Friedman 2006). Is our identity in crisis?

Psychoanalysis today is felt by many to be under existential threat and in need of protection. However, opinions differ both about what we need to protect and what we need to protect ourselves from. In terms of what we are protecting, the focus is often on aspects of the setting, perhaps because it has been so difficult to specify what should take place within the setting. For some, the focus is a certain level of frequency of sessions, but there is no consensus about what the minimum frequency should be. Other issues to do with the setting might include use of the couch, or not; willingness to offer virtual sessions, or not; and so on.

If we ask what defines a specifically psychoanalytic approach, again, there is no consensus (e.g. Fulgencio 2020; Green 2005; Karbelnig 2022). For some, evenly suspended attention and free association remain central, while others regard these technical devices as outmoded and even authoritarian. Should the analyst’s attention be focused exclusively on the transference, and if not, how does one determine when a transference interpretation is appropriate or helpful? To what extent should the analyst’s countertransference be taken into account, and is it better seen as a source of information about the patient, or about the analyst? If it is taken into account, how should it be made use of? Is interpretation still central to psychoanalytic technique or is the relationship with the analyst the engine of therapeutic change? Is the aim of analysis to cure, or merely to investigate? Is it still relevant to think in terms of an intrapsychic unconscious, or is it better to think in terms of a relational or bipersonal field? And so on.

responseSet.title: The Intolerance of Controversy in Psychoanalytic Organizations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Intolerance of Controversy in Psychoanalytic Organizations

Kenneth Eisold

Kenneth Eisold

New York City, USA
ken@keneisold.com

Kenneth Eisold  

Organizational role, occupational hazards, collaboration

In an age when our survival as a profession is less threatened but our growth increasingly depends upon our ability to apply our theories and techniques away from the couch, it has become important to understand how our organizational life relies on competing roles and different demands. That can be confusing for those of us who understand conflict primarily in the dyadic relationships of traditional psychoanalysis. Roles and collaboration, essential to work in organizations, require a greater acceptance of differences. Moreover, it requires a better understanding that our motives often stem from the tasks assigned in organizations and those tasks require collaboration rather than confrontation. A better understanding of these tensions and conflicts, endemic to organizational life, will enhance our ability to apply our insights outside our consultation rooms. And it offers, as well, the opportunity to work more effectively together on our professional organizations and associations, avoiding the bitter controversies that continue to bedevil our organizational life.

responseSet.title: Interpretation: The Interface between Internal Reality and External Reality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interpretation: The Interface between Internal Reality and External Reality

Eike Hinze

Eike Hinze

e.f.hinze@t-online.de

Eike Hinze  

Moderator: Gabriel Sapisochin (Spain). Panellists: Antonio Pérez-Sánchez (Spain). Eva Papiasvilli (USA). Roosevelt Cassiora (Brazil)

Most analysts would agree that their role in the analytic process is one of interpretation. The analyst offers himself as a container for all verbal and non-verbal utterances of a patient in the session. With this basic analytic attitude he tries to reach the patient’s unconscious internal world via his interpretations. However, when early traumatic emotional experiences could not be translated into symbolic verbal thought but remained in the soma, not accessing the psychic register, the analyst’s task becomes difficult. How can the somatic memory be activated?

This is the point of departure for Antonio Pérez-Sánchez’s case presentation (‘Interpretation and Primitive Body as External Reality of the Mind’). He writes at the onset:

And that this somatic memory can be activated, non-verbally of course, in the analytic situation. But we should pay more attention to the body as the handler of emotional life. And this occurs when early traumatic events have left such an imprint that introjection of the experience was impossible, because it was unbearable. In consequence, the body ceases to be a complement to the mind, and is instead dissociated, becoming a reality that is external to psychic life, as the only way to avoid the unbearable experience. Thus, it remains a primitive, unevolved body.

Very empathically, he describes the analysis of a patient whose main manifest symptomatology consisted in frequent attacks of binge eating. At the beginning these somatic manifestations had no psychic significance for the patient. Antonio Pérez-Sánchez convincingly demonstrates how he slowly reached the deeper somatic, non-symbolized levels of the patient’s psychopathology. He shows that interpretations encompass not only the verbal content of spoken sentences, but also the affective quality, the sound and the prosody of the analyst’s speech, including his silences. By means of his very sensitive and intuitive interpretations he was more and more able to gain access to the deepest layers in the patient’s psyche-soma. An important aspect in the analyst’s approach is the use of countertransference as well as working with the concept of mutual enactments; for example, he once gave in to the urgent demands of the patient to increase the frequency of sessions. This enactment helped him to understand his being identified with a non-empathic, over-feeding mother.

responseSet.title: Panel on: “Terror at the Gates: On Psychoanalysis and Fascism Amnesia”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel on: “Terror at the Gates: On Psychoanalysis and Fascism Amnesia”

Noëlle McAfee

Noëlle McAfee

noelle.c.mcafee@emory.edu

Noëlle McAfee  

Chair: Jill Salberg, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research

Presenters: Jill Salberg, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Carla Fischer Canessa, Asociación Psicoanalítica de Santiago (APSAN) and the Latin American Institute of Mental Health and Human Rights (ILAS); Andreas Hamburger, International Psychoanalytic University Berlin

Reporter: Noëlle McAfee, Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute

This panel considered the resurgence of authoritarianism as a manifestation of “repetition and denial of historical memory.” The presenters reflected on phenomena of forgetting the history of fascism and denying it even in the midst of its resurgence. While the stated theme was the novel concept of fascist amnesia, the underlying thread was about in whom transgenerational phenomena can trigger failures to think, especially when the content is unbearable.

Jill Salberg (Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research) catalogued recent elements of the resurgence of fascism in the United States but noted that despite having “been here before,” people seem “to have forgotten fascism,” even when it is glaringly manifest in their midst. Salberg drew on various theories to help understand the phenomenon, including Judith Herman’s concept of “episodic forgetting,” Vamik Volkan’s idea of “chosen traumas,” Maurice Apprey’s notion of “errands,” and Abraham and Torok’s theory of how phantoms from a previous generation can become encrypted in the present. All point to the phenomenon of transgenerational transmission of trauma, where the unfinished work or wounds of one generation get passed down to the next.

One key to breaking this transmission, Salberg notes, is survivors bearing witness to what has happened, especially in a way that inhabits the depth and grief at work. Yet this requires more – namely, successive generations engaged in a process of “continuing mentalizing.” Witnessing “needs to be in the service of defying and refusing fascistic amnesia.” We must “promote a kind of witnessing that touches us, moves us internally where empathic resonance is stirred, where grief and guilt may reside and we can enact a conscious engaged response.

IPA Congress, Cartagena 2003: Community Model – Challenging Times in Mind – with Communities Around the World in Mind

Debra Gill

Debra Gill

debra.gill@gmail.com

Debra Gill  

Reflective network therapy, trauma, attachment, play therapy

Chair: Talia Hatzor

Presenters: Shirley Hiscock, Carla Rentrop, Kathryn McCormick

Talia Hatzor introduced the panel members, each of whom, as members of the International Psychoanalytical Association Education Committee, has adapted contemporary psychoanalytic models, successfully creating programmes and educational opportunities in community settings. The work of each panelist demonstrates – to use Shirley Hiscock’s quote, from an interview with Betty Joseph in Encounters Through Generations (Joseph 2010) – “There is something right about psychoanalysis.” Hiscock, expanding on Joseph’s idea that psychoanalysis is attacked because its basic tenets garnish attention, states that regardless of powerful controversies, analytic concepts are recognizable and useful to many. Psychoanalysis stirs emotional experience that is paradoxically disquieting and familiar. Shirley Hiscock, Carla Rentrop and Kathryn McCormick each demonstrate the portability of the psychoanalytic setting (Hatzor 2020) and how analytic principles can be used to “hold” diverse populations in community settings, especially in schools.

The programmes discussed by the panelists are designated to expand the capacity of community settings to reach vulnerable, at-risk children. Hiscock describes international settings that offer psychoanalytic support as a public mission designed to educate parents, teachers and mental health professionals. Rentrop presents her clinical research findings from her work through the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR) and Clinical Center in New York City. Rentrop has been a Director, for over 20 years, of the IPTAR school-based programme that has offered psychoanalytically based treatment to ethnically and culturally diverse inner-city children in grades K–12 whose school performance and personality development have been impacted by traumatic histories and the experience of loss. Individual and group therapy informed by psychoanalytic theories of attachment, development and the study of narcissistic states, intersubjectivity and trauma are offered. The programme has also served as a training site for Institute candidates. McCormick presented her work of more than two decades in Tulalip, a community that has suffered the brutally forced and systemic process of

responseSet.title: Hypochondriacal Fear of Breast Cancer – A Failure of the Dual Maternal Functions: “Mother-of-the-Infant” and “Censorship of the Lover”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hypochondriacal Fear of Breast Cancer – A Failure of the Dual Maternal Functions: “Mother-of-the-Infant” and “Censorship of the Lover”

Denny Panitz PHD and Fotis Bobos

Fotis Bobos

Vas. Amalias 5B,14561, Kifissia, Athens, Greece
depanitz@hotmail.com

Peloponnissou 16, 15234 Halandri, Athens, Greece
bobosfotis@gmail.com

Fotis Bobos  

Hypochondria, trauma, breast, negative hallucination, maternal function, Paris psychosomatic school, Michel Fain, psychic bisexuality

Fain’s theoretical conceptualisation of the ‘Censorship of the Lover’ (1971) offers a framework to investigate the unrepresented traumatic aspects of the hypochondriacal fear of breast cancer. The failure of the maternal role to function as both the mother-of-the-infant and the lover-of-the-father creates considerable deficits in the primal psychosomatic tie. The authors aim to draw attention on to the importance of the mother-of-the-infant facet of the dual maternal function. The threatening repetitive scenario experienced by the hypochondriacal patient is considered as a form of pathological autoerotism, indicating an insufficient construction of psychic bisexuality, and, subsequently, of sexual identity. The hypochondriacal fear of breast cancer constitutes a positive hallucination whereas the denial of the healthy breast represents a negative hallucination (Green, 1993). The topos of the body onto which the fear of death is projected implies the existence of underlying associations related to the subject’s history. The complexities surrounding such acute hypochondriacal anxieties are demonstrated in the analysis of a female patient, during which the analytic dyad was called upon to disclose and construct different levels of meaning in order to enhance the capacity for mentalization.

responseSet.title: Desexualization: An interesting problem in The Ego and the Id
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Desexualization: An interesting problem in The Ego and the Id

Dominique Scarfone

Dominique Scarfone

1430, Redpath Crescent, Montréal (QC) H3G 1A2, Canada
dscarfone@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-5709-5077

Dominique Scarfone  

Desexualization, libido, death drive

The sudden appearance of the term “desexualization” in The Ego and the Id is considered as a marker of the subtle, almost unnoticeable changes that occurred in Freud's thinking after 1920. The strict dichotomy between life and death drives posed a series of new problems that force Freud to invoke a “desexualized libido” in order to restore some fluidity in the psychic apparatus. But the mechanism of desexualization was difficult to describe and Freud seems to resort to a circular explanation. In the end, the restored dialectics between Eros and the death drive, thanks to desexualization, force Freud to invoke a split in the ego itself.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Significance of the Ego in “The Ego and the Id” and its Unfulfilled Promise

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

Newton Centre, 246 Eliot Street, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467,USA
drfredbusch@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1457-3198

Fred Busch  

Unconscious ego, unconscious resistances, ego psychology

It is not well known that The Ego and the Id, where Freud presented his second model of the mind, and introduced a new role for the Ego, was ignored by many of the major theorists that followed. I will attempt to demonstrate the importance of this new view of the ego for clinical psychoanalysis, and what has been lost by its being ignored.

[[RunningHead]]

The Role of the Patient-Analyst Match in the Process and Outcome of Psychoanalysis, by Judy Kantrowitz, London and New York, Routledge, 2020, xxviii+342pp., $38.95, ISBN 9780367483500

Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

8109 Horseshoe Lane, Potomac, Maryland, USA
richard.waugaman@georgetown.edu

Richard Waugaman  

This misleadingly titled book is essentially an extended refutation of the outmoded view of analysis Kantrowitz was taught in the 1960s, that assumed any competent analyst should be able to treat any analysable patient. She convincingly demonstrates the wide range of crucial interactions between the individual dynamics of the patient and the analyst, sometimes adding the role of the supervisor. While we never want to burden a patient with irrelevant self-disclosures that shift the focus from the patient to the analyst, she shows how frequently the analyst's personal vulnerabilities are stirred by the patient's material. As she puts it, “The subtle impact of the interaction of the analyst's and patient's character and residual conflicts on the analytic process is not an aspect of our work that we can teach easily. Only with difficulty can we recognize and monitor it” (141).

The book is based on 15 previous articles by the author, plus an introduction which is new. Kantrowitz shares an enormous amount of her valuable clinical wisdom, with vivid vignettes. What moved me the most was her detailed account of the impact on her of her 31-year-old son's death from brain cancer, five years after it was diagnosed. It is almost unbearably painful to accompany the author on her journey, as her personal burden inevitably influenced her clinical work. She candidly acknowledges that she ultimately realized it was her wish to deny her son's poor prognosis that led her to want to “protect” her patients from knowing about his illness. Kantrowitz writes critically of the many analysts who never disclose their fatal illnesses to their patients, depriving those patients of being able to say goodbye. David Feinsilver, who wrote movingly of working the last few years of his life with a fatal cancer, told me it is impossible to predict how one will react to being given a fatal prognosis. Analysts, being human, sometimes have no alternative than to resort to denial when they are dying.

Kantrowitz also shares a powerful vignette where she repeatedly dreamed about a certain patient. Perplexed, she thought of returning to her former analyst. Only then did she understand what her patient was eliciting from her unconscious. When he was four, he had been left alone with his two-year-old brother, who fell out a window and sustained brain damage. Kantrowitz finally connected this with her own mother's miscarriage when she was four, and for the first time she derepressed her hostile feelings toward that potential sibling. She extrapolates that the analyst's emotional engagement and vulnerability in every analysis is necessary for optimal therapeutic action for the patient.
