Ascoltare con tutti i sensi (estensioni del paradigma dell’ascolto psicoanalitico) [Listen with all Senses (Extensions of the Paradigm of Psychoanalytical Listening).] by T. Bastianini, A. Ferruta, B. Guerrini Degl’Innocenti, Rome, Giovanni Fioriti Editore, 2021, pp.130, Euro 16,00, ISBN 9788836250431

Giuseppe Di Chiara

Giuseppe Di Chiara

giuseppe.di.chiara.38@gmail.com

Giuseppe Di Chiara  

New patients, new pathologies, renewed instruments in the psychoanalytical arsenal, these are the ingredients of this book, so Stefano Bolognini informs the reader in his preface. The exploration of such a vast field requires – he adds – “psychoanalysts of advanced frontiers”, such as the three authors, who divide the task between themselves into three distinct parts.

Tiziana Bastianini begins with the study of the emergence of the contents of the unconscious in its heterogeneous forms, when the psychic intensity is unable to be contained or expressed in words. There are psychic forces that cannot express themselves in representations of ideas and words. They employ other expressive means, both in the patient – acting, somatizations, gestures – and in the analyst – also acting, somatizations and countertransference problems.

What to do? Broaden metapsychology, listen not only to words, but also to bodies in their psychosomatic totality. Hence there is a type of listening, no longer just with the ears, but also with the body: the incarnate listening. New ideas and instruments have already enriched psychoanalytical research, of which the author provides an accurate list and presentation of the well-known authors, both in Italy and in the psychoanalytical oecumene, giving the reader concise and precise information.

To achieve what? To allow the illumination of the unconscious as much as possible, and hence to rediscover words and those parts which have been lost due to repression (repressed unconscious), and to express in words those parts which still must be found for the first time (non-repressed unconscious). It is significant that in order to achieve this, Bastianini, as with the other authors, not only utilizes the contribution from many colleagues and research, but also makes continual reference to Freud and his works: from the early Freud, that of the Project for a Scientific Psychology, passing though his entire output, to the Results, Ideas, Problems, always with enrichments and elaborations.

The Seminar. Book IV: The Object Relation (1956–57) by Jacques Lacan, edited by Jacques-Alain Miller, translated by A. R. Price, Cambridge and Medford MA, Polity Press, 2020, 462 pp., £30.00 (hardcopy), ISBN: 9780745660356

Dany Nobus

Dany Nobus

dany.nobus@brunel.ac.uk

Dany Nobus  

For a period of ten years, between November 1953 and November 1963, Jacques Lacan delivered a weekly seminar at Sainte-Anne Hospital in Paris, as part of the psychoanalytic training program of the Société française de Psychanalyse. Each academic year, Lacan chose to present 393 his seminar on a different topic, while still ensuring that the clinical and technical issues of psychoanalysis took pride of place. In addition, although the seminar was exclusively directed at psychoanalysts in training, the lectures were open to all interested parties and were therefore regularly attended by emerging and established scholars, as well as researchers without a psychoanalytic background who did not intend to enter the psychoanalytic profession. From January 1964 until June 1980, the seminar was no longer part of a psychoanalytic training program, which enabled Lacan to explore a broader range of questions, and which also contributed to the massive expansion of his audience.

Each weekend prior to the delivery of his seminar, which was consistently scheduled on Wednesdays at 12.15pm, Lacan carefully prepared a set of notes that would constitute the backbone for his lecture, without therefore depriving himself of the joy of improvisation and the occasional unplanned digression. From January 1953, Lacan also started to employ a stenographer, which provided him with the opportunity to read (and sometimes correct) what he had effectively said and which made it easier for him to use his seminar as the basis for a journal article or book chapter.

Until the early 1970s, Lacan himself had never given much thought to publishing his seminars. However, during the early summer of 1972, Jacques-Alain Miller, a philosopher who had actively participated in most of Lacan’s seminars since January 1964 and who had married Lacan’s daughter Judith, decided to edit and release Lacan’s seminars in book format, with the goal of coming as close as possible to what Lacan had said (Miller 1985, 16–17; 2011/2021, 96). Lacan left it to Miller to choose where to start and the latter decided to commence with Seminar XI, because it was the first lecture-course he himself had attended (Miller 2011/2021, 96). When the book was published, early in 1973, Miller stated in a short editor’s note that, from now on, this would be the only “authentic version … [of] the original, which does not exist” (Miller 1973/1977, xi). In a lengthy afterword, Lacan himself did not hesitate to assert that the editor was the actual author of the text, because it was Miller who had invented the written form and who had allowed the spoken words to become readable (Lacan 1973/2012).1 Lacan was delighted with the result and so Miller soon became the one and only official (legal) “establisher” of Lacan’s seminars.

The Body in the Psychoanalytic Experience. Between Freud, Lacan and Winnicott, by Noemí Lustgarten de Canteros, Santiago de Chile, Pólvora Editorial, 2020 First Reprint, 320pp, $6.99 (International E-book), ISBN: 978-956-9441-21-9

Rodrigo Jerez

Rodrigo Jerez

Santiago, CP, Chile
rojasjerez@icloud.com

Rodrigo Jerez  

This is the kind of book I would have liked to have read during my studies as a psychologist and while training as a psychoanalyst. As Lustgarten points out in her text:

Throughout the book, the enquiry centres on how to subtly refine the practice of psychoanalysis today, taking into account the contemporary world and its effects on the patients who consult us, and on ourselves. To this end, I propose a perspective that I encounter in Winnicott, which distances itself from the identity dilemma between novel and orthodox approaches in psychoanalysis (22–23).

Thus, the issues reviewed in the book traverse not only different periods of the author’s clinical practice, but also different dimensions of a history of metapsychology and clinical psychoanalysis in Latin America and particularly in Argentina. It could undoubtedly be said that the journey undertaken is not exhaustive, or that it does not meet the requirements of an encyclopaedic study. However, in the opinion of the author of this review, this is precisely the value of the path of enquiry in the text under review.

The title unfolds in all its depth on this point. The words “experience” and “body” are pivotal to the entire work, not only proposing an embodied psychoanalysis, made incarnate through its practice, but also taking Winnicott as a starting point for a psychoanalysis that is centred on the conditions that have shaped the subject’s experience, both in the contemporary world and during a session. This challenge questions the reader both in their own clinical experience and in the metapsychological interest aroused by the psychoanalytic session described in its intimacy – all this from a conceptual review based on the central axes within any of the various theoretical psychoanalytic scenarios.

The text is initially organized around angst and its functioning both in the session and in the theories of Freud, Lacan and Winnicott. A second section begins with the presence and reinstatement of the body within the analytic scene, since this incorporation actually comes from physical suffering as a psychosomatic expression of human existence. A third section is structured around the articulation of transference as a privileged territory for the unfolding of an experience of physical suffering that seeks its transformation in a session, an experience based on Winnicott’s idea of the transitional. Winnicott’s “play” is combined with the ideas presented by Sandor Ferenczi in his text on child-analysis with adults. That is, they meet in a territory where the analyst’s work consists of the transformation from the patient’s incapacity for play towards building a capacity for play, as well as the space that opens up to understanding the conditions for initiating the capacity for play, this transitionality and experience perhaps being opened up for the first time with the patient in the transference field. The volume closes with a description of transitionality as the terrain of physical intimacy.

Evolutive Ruptures. Psychoanalysis of Breakdowns and Defensive Solutions. by Anna Maria Nicolò, 2021, Milano, Raffaello Cortina Editore, € 24, ISBN: 978-88-3285-320-9

Massimo Vigna-Taglianti

Massimo Vigna-Taglianti

m.vignataglianti@gmail.com

Massimo Vigna-Taglianti  

Evolutive Ruptures. Psychoanalysis of Breakdowns and Defensive Solutions is a book that, already from its title, presents a distinct originality and precise theoretical connotation, with considerable consequent effects on the clinical and technical implications dealt with in its constituent chapters.

The title of the volume in fact expresses what may seem at face value to be an oxymoron, as it aligns two terms in apparent antithesis: the first refers to the fracture of an object’s intactness (since it implies serious damage to the structure) or alternatively to the interruption of a bond, of a relationship; the other term instead, in its most literal meaning, refers to the concept of progress, of development, of unfolding.

In reality this antinomy intercepts and transmits – and herein lies the originality of this generous contribution dedicated to adolescence and its typical psychopathological manifestations – the dialectic tension, implicit in the concept of the evolutive breakdown, between a destructive drive that produces a solution of continuity in the mental functioning of a subject in development, and psychic movement that is in a certain measure orderly and directed towards a creative finale. Such a tension is the thread, clearly evident, that winds through all the theoretical considerations illustrated in the diverse chapters of the book, vividly supported by an exhaustive clinical exemplification.

I have begun this review with this apparently irreconcilable antinomy – comparable in a certain sense to the tectonic clash between two continental masses, infancy and adulthood, or the psychotic and neurotic parts of the personality – to highlight how the spirit that permeates Anna Maria Nicolò’s work is in itself authentically psychoanalytic; in fact, all of Freud’s teachings of knowing how to transform obstacles and disturbances along the bumpy path of psychoanalytic research in successive theoretical and technical cardinal turning points move in this same direction. So it was for transference and countertransference and, more recently, for enactment.

Le Schizophrène en Mal D'objet*, by Vassilis Kapsambelis, Paris, PUF, coll. Le fil rouge, 2020, €25, ISBN 978-2-13 08-2275-2

Anne Tirilly

Anne Tirilly

atirilly@orange.fr

Anne Tirilly  

Following on from many previous works exploring psychoanalytic thought in psychiatry, Vassilis Kapsambelis has examined in his latest book the therapeutic relationship between a schizophrenic patient and the object of investment that their therapist proposes to become for them. To do this, he relies both on a rich clinical experience recounted with as much vivacity as simplicity, and on a subtle metapsychological analysis. Beginning with a rigorous definition of the specificities of schizophrenia, he then reflects on the qualities of an “object for schizophrenic” without losing sight of Freudian propositions. The book is interspersed with clinical case histories, some of which span several decades, illustrating theoretical developments by way of thoughtful and well-known accounts. The author concludes his reflection with a plea for a topography that takes into account the object as co-author of the construction of the subject.

First and foremost, Kapsambelis specifies the particularities of schizophrenia, contrary to the contemporary tendency to group psychoses in a kind of extended and vague spectrum. *The schizophrenic in need of an object.596

Désir, Douleur, Pensée. Masochisme Originaire et Théorie Psychanalytique,* by Marilia Aisenstein, Paris, Éditions d’Ithaque, 2020, 112p., €18, ISBN: 9782490350100

Gilbert Diatkine

Gilbert Diatkine

gilbert.diatkine@wanadoo.fr

Gilbert Diatkine  

Marilia Aisenstein is the European representative of the International New Groups (ING) Committee of the International Psychoanalytic Association, of which she was Vice-President. She has also been the President of the Psychoanalytic Society of Paris, of which she is a full member and training analyst. In addition she is a member of the Hellenic Society of Psychoanalysis, and a full member and training analyst of the Pierre Marty Paris Institute of Psychosomatics. Many of the clinical accounts she presents in her new book, Désir, douleur, pensée. Masochisme originaire et théorie psychanalytique, come from her experience as a psychoanalyst with patients with serious physical illnesses.

These clinical accounts, always remarkable, alternate with summaries from literary texts. Literature and clinical practice inform each other, and help readers to understand the new ideas that the author brings to psychoanalytic theory. Unlike neurotic patients, the patients with severe somatic conditions whom Marilia Aisenstein treats psychoanalytically do not complain about the impossibility of fulfilling their desires. They simply avoid desiring, protecting themselves against the objects that could excite them.

At the age of 32, Carla has already had surgery for breast cancer and has had two strokes. She is a professional sportswoman, who has no love life, and who fell ill when she had to stop her intensive physical training following a fracture. After a year of psychotherapy, she reports a first dream: “a landscape of snow, neither cold nor hot”. She then reveals that she has lost her sense of cold and heat since her first period at the age of 12. Her analyst points out that she has been anaesthetized. Carla then speaks for the first time about the death of her father, which occurred at the same time, as well as the mistreatment she suffered from her mother, who beat her and received her lovers in Carla’s presence. It took Carla’s anaesthetized pain to turn into suffering, and into masochistic representations, for the process to begin. It is necessary to read Marilia Aisenstein’s book to follow the exciting analytic process that begins with this session and will last eight years (37–41).

responseSet.title: Fear, Loss and Disconnection: The Emotional Impact of the Covid-19 Pandemic upon Staff Working in Mental Health Services and How the Organization Can Help - A Psychoanalytic Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fear, Loss and Disconnection: The Emotional Impact of the Covid-19 Pandemic upon Staff Working in Mental Health Services and How the Organization Can Help - A Psychoanalytic Perspective

Jo O’Reilly

Jo O’Reilly

London, UK
jooreillyread@gmail.com

Jo O’Reilly  

NHS Mental Health Trust, Organisational containment of Anxiety, Covid-19 Pandemic

This paper describes the work of a psychoanalyst working within NHS mental health services in the UK . The central contribution of a psychoanalytic approach within psychiatric care in offering a committed attempt towards understanding the patients’ presentation, rather than treatment primarily aimed at symptom control, is described. Beyond this, the specific contribution of psychoanalytic ideas in establishing a containing framework for staff, and how this strengthens the capacity of the organisation as a whole to contain anxiety and to metabolize complex projective processes is outlined. Examples are given with clinical illustrations of activities which enhance this capacity in ordinary times.

The author then turns to the impact of the covid-19 pandemic upon staff and patients, describing how fear, threat and experiences of multiple losses have permeated all areas of our lives and activated primitive defences. The pandemic starkly revealed profoundly disturbing questions about our assumptions and habits, adding to the intensity and multi-layered quality of the anxieties evoked . Urgent attention has been drawn to our deeply problematic relationship with the natural world, our own habitat, and indefensible social inequities have been crudely exposed. Staff have been caught between their own fear, the need to contain increased disturbance in their patients, already struggling with fragmented and disordered states of mind, and pressures from an organisation under intense strain. The capacity of mental health staff to act as containers for their patient’s distress has been profoundly challenged and compromised.

This paper outlines how the pandemic has highlighted the crucial role of the organisation as a container for anxiety and in supporting staff to do their work in mental health care. In order to strengthen this capacity during the crisis, the author describes how ideas derived from psychoanalytic principles were developed into guidance for NHS Mental Health Trusts during the early days of the pandemic . This guidance was adopted nationally by the Royal College of Psychiatrists and is summarised in this paper.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Emma Lunbeck

Emma Lunbeck

University of Chicago

Emma Lunbeck  

To the Editor,

I thank Dr Rusbridger for his response. However, I would like to correct what seems to me a misreading of my paper. I do not wish to suggest that the work of the analyst is to solve riddles. In fact, just the opposite: I believe that there are particular moments in an analysis when the work of interpretation is caricatured or reduced, by the patient or by the analyst, to the task of solving riddles. I wanted to think more deeply about these moments - about how these scenarios emerge between patient and analyst and how we might respond so that the analytic work can recover its true proportions, its healing dimensions. Rusbridger’s paper seemed to me to offer a vivid example of this predicament: a moment of extreme pressure towards interpretation (which I characterize as a moment of “meeting the Sphinx”). In such an atmosphere, the analyst’s words may not function as interpretation, but as riddle solving. I am not trying to denude the analyst’s function, as Rusbridger suggests in his reply, but rather to point out the very real constraint on interpretation that may emerge at just this moment.

774

Affect, Representation and Language. Between the Silence and the Cry, by Howard B. Levine. London and New York: Routledge, 2022. 155pp, £31.99 (paperback), ISBN 9780367774318.

Endre Koritar

Endre Koritar

elkoritar@gmail.com

Endre Koritar  

Psychoanalysis as theory and praxis has provided a new tool for clinicians treating patients with mental health challenges since Freud’s writings on the subject over 100 years ago. But while psychoanalytic theories of thinking have been usefully applied to the treatment of neurotic disorders, they have been of limited value in the treatment of more regressed patients with limit1 conditions between neurosis and psychosis such as personality disorders, addictions, post-traumatic stress disorder, autistic spectrum disorders and psychoses. Psychoanalytic technical approaches have had limited success in the above conditions since they are generally based on epistemological principles relying heavily on verbal symbolic interventions whereas the underlying dynamics of regressed patients have a more primal pre-verbal origin often not amenable to dynamic explications of unconscious phantasy. In some cases, the application of standard technique and interpretation of unconscious phantasy may lead to regression and symptomatic exacerbation.

Patients with non-neurotic internal structural dynamics often require different kinds of therapeutic approaches than patients with neurotic structure and dynamics. Whereas the latter are often amenable to verbal, archaeological approaches uncovering repressed unconscious phantasy, the former often require the introduction of parameters that may render the regressed patient more amenable to analytic therapy (Eissler 1953).

Levine provides the reader with a theoretical framework applicable to clinical work with limit cases that is both a thorough review of the literature of the field and his integrated comprehensive, technical approach. The challenge for the clinician working with these types of case is to arrive at a representation of phenomena that present themselves in the consulting room as repetitions of past unmentalized actual experiences and are acted upon without the possibility of symbolic representation since the traumatic nidus of the experience had been elided defensively from the “representational imperative” (23, footnote) and remained as beta elements not yet digested by alpha function.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report, IPA Congress Cartagena 2023: Concrete Patients – a Challenge for the Psychoanalyst’s Mind

Maria Perletti

Maria Perletti

cristinaperletti@inwind.it

Maria Perletti  

Chair: Benedetta Guerrini Degl’Innocenti

Presenters: Lawrence J. Brown

Sara Boffito

Celia Fix Korbivcher

After an introduction to the topic by Benedetta Guerrini Degl’Innocenti, Lawrence J. Brown begins to discuss his work entitled “On Psychic Flatness”. He describes two kinds of “psychic flatness”: the concreteness shown by patients with Asperger disease (a condition included in autism spectrum disorders, ASD) and that presented by patients with psychosomatic disorders. Brown highlights how both conditions, usually not considered related, and even in their mutual differences, show disturbances of the representative capacity and in the emotional experience, with a dulling of emotions. These patients reveal an inability in the comprehension of the emotional experience and, in addition and accordingly, concrete thinking. This element of concreteness is common to apparently unrelated clinical situations, yet the common element is the imprisonment of the concrete patient, who, as expressed by Brown, “casts a shadow of narrow meaning across all experience”. In the primary relationship of the future ASD child, there is the experience of a premature tearing of the infant from the mother: this emotionally overloads the child and, despite attempts, overwhelms him or her, thereby determining a flatness of the infantile psyche with the feeling of being alone and threatened in a dangerous world. Brown underlines the importance of living an emotionally meaningful life, of developing the ability to represent emotional experience. When this representative function does not develop sufficiently, one is left in a flat world of literal facts, limited in the capacity of psychic growth. This psychic situation determines what André Green calls “hypercathexis of the factual and of reality”, as it is possible to observe in psychosomatic patients and, according to what Brown adds, also in ASD patients. In keeping with Freud’s thought, there is a “puzzling leap” in the transformation of a somatic stimulus in psychic experience. Green introduces the concept of a somato-psychic frontier, where somatic excitation is transformed into an emotional experience. Brown suggests that in both psychosomatic and ASD patients there is a block at the level of the somato-psychic frontier, and proposes that both conditions fall under the wording of “flattening of existence”: assuming that anything emotionally unelaborated causes an absence of dimensionality, and influences the ability to experience deeper emotional meaning.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in the Age of Totalitarianism edited by Matt Ffytche and Daniel Pick. Published by Routledge, London and New York, 2016; 287 pp; £120 (hardback); £31.99 (paperback)

David Morgan

David Morgan

Drdhmorgan@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2127-4492

David Morgan  

This book opens with a dedication to the Cambridge historian philosopher Professor John Forrester, whose contribution to research and writings in psychoanalysis and history laid some of the foundations for thinking about the role of the unconscious in political and social life. He was able to combine these complex features in expanding and developing Freud’s thinking in “Civilisation and its Discontents” into an exploration of the specific and unique political and social unconscious influences that go into creating an individual’s life. As we can see from this book, this area continues to be fertile ground for psychoanalytic investigation. This beautifully edited collection continues in this rich vein, focusing on a deep exploration of the psychoanalytic understanding of totalitarianism, in particular its ubiquitous rise and its ever present attractions and seductions, both in the individual psyche and in those who govern.

Daniel Pick and Matt Fytche are aware that psychoanalysis grew out of the same post-sociopolitical and post-colonial melting pot as the rest of European culture, recognizing that it is fundamentally Eurocentric and has suffered in the past from racism. But as they demonstrate, the enormous exposure to the depredations of the Second World War, and the horrors of the Holocaust, Stalin’s progroms, the assassination of J. F. Kennedy and, I would add, Martin Luther King have created a huge psychoanalytic questioning of how psychology can also be used by totalitarian regimes, be they on the Left or the Right.

When we think of propaganda, we think of Goebbels Big Lie, and knowing psychological manipulation of the masses, but psychoanalytic ideas were blatantly used in America by a nephew of Freud’s, Edward Bernays, who used his uncle’s ideas to help him convince the public that bacon and eggs was the true all-American breakfast. Commercial manipulation through advertising was redolent with psychoanalytic theory and indeed involved psychoanalysts. Think of the series Madmen. More demonically psychoanalytic ideas have been used in the instruments of totalitarianism as in re-education programmes and brainwashing during the Cold War in both the USSR and America, as illustrated in the shocking chapter in this book by “Knuth Müller: Psychoanalysis and American Intelligence Since 1940: Unexpected Liaisons”. We have to acknowledge here that even Freud, despite his family’s treatment by the Gestapo and the loss of members of his family, was reluctant to decouple entirely from German psychoanalysis as it continued to be practised in Nazi Germany.

Toward a Unified Theory of Psychoanalysis: Foundation in a Revised and Expanded Ego Psychology, by Morris N. Eagle. London: Routledge, 2022, $42.95 (paperback), 342pp. ISBN 9780367767532.

Henry Smith

Henry Smith

17 Hammond Street, Cambridge, MA 02138-1915, USA
henryfsmith@gmail.com

Henry Smith  

Morris Eagle has devoted a lifetime to the literature on psychoanalysis and psychology and the links between the two. His latest book is a passionate argument for an integrated and unified theory of mind, based on an expanded foundation in ego psychology. In the tradition of Hartmann and Rappaport, Eagle holds that psychoanalytic theory must be consistent with findings from other disciplines to communicate with them. Ego psychology, he argues, is the logical bridge to other approaches to the mind, notably to research in cognitive psychology and the neurosciences.

But his goal is more ambitious. The final chapter of his book begins: “A basic assumption motivating this book is that the project of seeking integration of different psychoanalytic schools and developing a relatively unified psychoanalytic theory of the mind is both possible and desirable” (273). The goal is important, but what is the best method for achieving it? Eagle’s method is to examine every school, including the origins and current practice of ego psychology, with a highly critical eye. But eventually we have to ask, who is his audience and who will accept this unified theory?

Eagle largely ignores previous approaches to integration; Rangell, for example, crusaded for years for a “total composite psychoanalytic theory”, and, currently, Solms bridges neuroscience and psychoanalysis. Both would seem pertinent to his project. He is sharply critical of those who argue for a theoretical “common ground” either of transference (Wallerstein 1990) or of countertransference (Gabbard 1995), and he gives no consideration to Sandler’s distinction between public and private theories and the “elasticity” of psychoanalytic terms that allow contradictory “partial theories” to “coexist happily” in the mind of the clinician as long as they remain descriptively unconscious (Sandler 1983, 36–8). Such partial clinical integrations, I suggest, are not only possible but also desirable and even inevitable as long as we do not pit one theory against another in a tendentious debate (Smith 2003a, 140).

John Rosegrant

John Rosegrant

410 N. Huron St., Ypsilanti MI, 48197
rosegrantj@gmail.com

John Rosegrant  

Psychoanalysis at its origin was saturated with literature. Freud’s literary engagement extended well beyond his familiar uses of the Oedipus myth and Hamlet to ground his concept of the nuclear complex, and beyond his efforts to psychoanalytically understand literary works and creative writers. He compared his written case histories to novels, and he was awarded the Goethe Prize for literature. But more than this, he imbued his writings with allusions to literature that helped to capture his psychoanalytic insights ranging from some (Shakespeare, Goethe, etc.) that will be familiar to most educated people today, to others that most of us would know nothing about without the help of Strachey’s footnotes. Already in Studies on Hysteria he refers to Goethe; The Interpretation of Dreams begins with 1142

Anne Tirilly  

On the occasion of his new book, Jean-Louis Baldacci continues his reflections following the thread of his work on sublimation, interpretation and clinical practice. He takes up these themes and articulates them with the issue of sexuality in all its paradoxicality. Indeed, the sexual is paradoxical in its very essence, since it “separates and unites, and is as uncomfortable with chaos as with any excessive arrangements likely to oppose its flexibility” (1).

The author relies on a wide-ranging clinical practice in a most precise and lively way, emanating from different frames in order to illustrate the emergence from splitting and the overcoming of the paradox. The hurdles characteristic of borderline situations in psychoanalysis 796

Karen Dougherty

Karen Dougherty

Toronto, Canada
karenbridget@gmail.com

Karen Dougherty  

3-LM, tracking patient change, clinical observation, working groups

The three-level model (3-LM) tracks transformation in psychoanalytic cases via a focused working group study of detailed clinical material. This Panel was a continuation of a series of programmes on the work of the Clinical Observation Committee held at previous IPA Congresses in Mexico City, Prague, Boston, Buenos Aires, London, and in an Online Congress during the pandemic. In this presentation, panellists summarized how the 3-LM worked, with two summarizing cases presented using this method at their Institute.

Coming to Life in the Consulting Room: Toward a New Analytic Sensibility, Thomas H. Ogden, London and New York, Routledge, 2022, 175pp., £29.99, ISBN: 978-1-032-13264-8

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

parsonstmc@gmail.com

Michael Parsons  

E.M. Forster described the poet C.P. Cavafy as a Greek gentleman in a straw hat, standing “at a slight angle to the universe” (Forster 1923, 91). This reminds me – minus the straw hat and Greekness – of how Thomas Ogden stands in relation to the psychoanalytic universe.

Coming to Life in the Consulting Room: Toward a New Analytic Sensibility (Ogden 2022) is an important book. Reading it increases one’s understanding of psychoanalysis – not just one’s knowledge, but one’s understanding of it. A principal theme is the difference between what Ogden calls “epistemological” and “ontological” psychoanalysis, the latter being the “new analytic sensibility” that the title refers to. This seems to me a significant conceptual advance. Ogden says, though, that he only recently found words to express this distinction (10). Clues to what he means by it accumulate through the book, as it seems they did for Ogden in his own thinking. The reader has to sidle up to the book in a similar way. It is no good coming at it head on; the angle from which it is written is part of it.

This book cannot be viewed in isolation from Ogden’s other writings. Its title alone shows that. “Coming to life”, and what it means not to be alive, is a thread running through Ogden’s work. It goes back at least to his paper “Analysing Forms of Aliveness and Deadness of the Transference-Countertransference”, published in 1995. It reappears in the title of his book Reclaiming Unlived Life: Experiences in Psychoanalysis (Ogden 2016). In Coming to Life in the Consulting Room, Ogden sees symptoms as “markers of ‘where the bodies are buried’, that is, where in the psyche and soma certain aspects of a patient’s sense of who she is and who she might become have been exiled and perhaps have died”. He goes on: “The ‘experience of being buried alive’ that I am referring to here is not to be equated with repression or dissociation … By contrast [it] involves loss of the sense of aliveness and ‘the feeling of real’” (4). This last phrase points us to Chapter 2, where Ogden links it to a reading of Winnicott’s paper “Communicating and Not Communicating Leading to a Study of Certain Opposites” (Winnicott 1965, 179–192). Ogden has a very particular way of reading the work of other analysts. Like Cavafy, he approaches them from his own angle. This reading of Winnicott is an example, and I shall come back to it when we know that angle a bit better.

responseSet.title: “No One Can Hate You More Than I Do”: The Perverse Interplay of Life and Death Drives in Roman Polanski’s Film Bitter Moon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“No One Can Hate You More Than I Do”: The Perverse Interplay of Life and Death Drives in Roman Polanski’s Film Bitter Moon

Michael Shoshani and Batya Shoshani

Batya Shoshani

Tel Aviv, Israel
mbshoshani@gmail.com

Jerusalem, Israel

Batya Shoshani  

Perversion, sadomasochism, life and death drives, eros and thanatos, psychoanalysis and film, interdisciplinary studies

In this paper, the authors explore the depiction of perversion and the associated interplay of life and death drives in Roman Polanski’s 1992 film Bitter Moon. To begin with, a theoretical discussion is presented regarding perverse organizations of mastery and sadomasochism. Perversion is viewed as an expression of the death drive under erotic disguise, in which the destructive fingerprint of the death drive is revealed at every stage, having as its ultimate purpose the destruction of the other. Based on these theoretical insights a dialogue is developed with Polanski’s film, which brings to life the theory of sadomasochistic relations through the multidimensional aesthetic medium of cinema. It is shown how Polanski’s cinematic oeuvre conveys the essence of the difficult and complex experience of perverse relations, where the life and death drives and their transformations are manifested. The portrayal of the sadomasochistic relations in this film contributes to the experiential knowledge with which the authors promote insight that would potentially enrich the clinical work with patients with perverse organizations.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: IPA Congress Cartagena 2023: Adolescents in the Line of Fire Today: Ideals–Identifications–Transformations

Gretchen Schmutz

Gretchen Schmutz

gschmutz@alumni.uchicago.edu

Gretchen Schmutz  

Chair: Christine Anzieu-Premmereur, French Psychoanalytical Association

Presenters: Christine Franckx, Belgian Psychoanalytical Society; Fernando Gṓmez, Argentine Psychoanalytic Association; Julie Augoyard, Paris Psychoanalytical Society

Discussant: Mary Brady, American Psychoanalytic Association

This panel considers the profound capacity of societal and environmental realities to threaten and interfere with the necessary identifications that allow adolescent transformation and evolving subjectivity. Following an introduction by Anzieu-Premmereur, Franckx examines the effects of the climate crisis on the emerging identity of the adolescent. She suggests that the process of mourning childhood and the loss of infantile omnipotence can be impeded by perilous realities. Franckx demonstrates that while the path towards self-differentiation requires increasing awareness of reality, threats to survival can derail the process and interfere with the containment that is necessary for psychological development.

Franckx focuses on the climate crisis and hypothesizes that catastrophic events can propel the adolescent towards an imbalance between drives and psychic defences, leading to regression and breakdown. Part of Franckx’s argumentation is that there is a breakdown in the function of groups in containing anxiety in the face of peril which creates a risk that the adolescent will not find sufficient containment to transform infantile omnipotence.

For the adolescent to make sense of the ordinary, expectable internal turmoil and to integrate this with the demands of external reality, Franckx suggests that the adolescent needs the freedom to explore and to revolt against authority as well as social and political ideals. These challenges against authority can also have importance for the collective and can lead to cultural, societal, and political changes. She argues that global warming presents a specific kind of challenge to the adolescent because there is no clear political structure or ideology to attack. The naturally emerging and deepening awareness of what is external and internal is interfered with by the ambiguity of the threat. With the climate crisis, the world does not feel solid enough to be challenged and to withstand the projections of the adolescent, so the fear of breakdown is projected into the future. She emphasizes, importantly, that the climate crisis is not a singular event or one with a timeline. Franckx suggests, “Therefore, the environmental crisis hits the delicate adolescent process of integrating internal and external realities because temporality itself, a necessary structuring beacon in times of psychic crisis, has become less reliable.”

CONTACT Gretchen A. Schmutz gschmutz@alumni.uchicago.edu

responseSet.title: Christian David
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Christian David

Laurent Danon-Boileau

Laurent Danon-Boileau

4 rue du Cloître Notre Dame, Paris 75004, France
ldanonboileau@gmail.com

Laurent Danon-Boileau  

Affect, anti-intellectualism, psychosomatics

This article reviews the essential themes of Christian David's work. The contribution of this French psychoanalyst is deeply rooted in anti-intellectualistic convictions and a definite opposition to Lacan. His contribution to psychoanalytical theory can be seen as a full recognition of the work of affect, even in terms of representation as well as a relative lessening of the role of language. It is original in the interpretation it proposes of psychosomatic disorders, due to the role his theory gives to affect in the sublimating intention of the drive. His fundamental convictions go hand in hand with a distrust of any position that would tend to privilege a systematic and ready-made description of the diversity of psychic life. For him dream is more a function than a content and the good assessment of ‘psychic bisexuality' opposes destiny’ while being the shibboleth of true sublimation.

[[RunningHead]]

Au bout du Rouleau. Récits Cliniques1, by Gérard Szwec, Paris, PUF, 2021, €14, ISBN: 2130827101

Denys Ribas

Denys Ribas

denysribas@free.fr

Denys Ribas  

Gérard Szwec is a referential author in psychosomatics and in particular in the field of working with children. His work is an extension of that of Pierre Marty and especially of Michel Fain, founders with Michel de M’Uzan and Christian David of a branch of psychosomatics called “L’École de Paris”. The originality of this School is to position somatic disorders not as meaningful symptoms, as in hysteria, but on the contrary as having the meaning of a failure of mentalization, testimony of a disorganization, which suddenly reaches the soma, sometimes seriously.

L’investigation psychosomatique (Marty, de M’Uzan and David 1963) sets out to evaluate the functionality of mentalization in hospitalized patients with somatic conditions. The clinical examples give an account of the limits of conflictualization and psychic symbolization, with a poverty of oneiric life and of fantasies, thought and behaviour becoming operational. The preconscious seems to have lost its thickness. And even anxiety can disappear in an essential depression. The therapeutic implications are important because classic psychoanalytic treatment then involves an element of danger, and face-to-face work is preferable with a more active verbal presence of the analyst.

We can advise those who are not familiar with the difference from other psychosomatic fields to start the book with the last chapter, which takes up the history of these ideas after Freud. It describes the creation of the “Paris School” and articulates its specificities in relation to “The Chicago School” around Franz Alexander in the 1930s, or to the works of Winnicott and, in France, those of Jean-Paul Valabrega. It also shows the differences between the thinking of Pierre Marty (1979) and Michel Fain. The former insists on the opposition between the organization and the disorganization of psychic functioning. In the failure of the psychic capacities of regression, especially formal regression, the disorganization is accentuated and is protected by fixations, which the analyst must preserve. Michel Fain draws out all the consequences of the second theory of the drives that accompanies the structural model.

[[RunningHead]]

PANEL REPORT, IPA Congress, Cartagena 2023: Paranoia, Claustrophobia and Musical Sublimation in the Time of War

Carla Rentrop

Carla Rentrop

carlarentrop@gmail.com

Carla Rentrop  

Chair: Clara Nemas de Urman

Presenters: Heinz Weiss, Susan Finkelstein, Francis Grier

The panel explored causes and understanding of war by focusing on Roger Money-Kyrle’s historical writings foreboding World War II and the fear of encirclement (H. Weiss); being swallowed, buried alive, encased within the object, persecuted, and annihilated (S. Finkelstein); and the creative, reparative use of music by two wartime composers (F. Grier).

In his paper, “The ‘Bolero’ of Propaganda and War: The relevance of Roger Money-Kyrle’s Contributions from the 1930s for Understanding the Current War of Russia against Ukraine,” Heinz Weiss drew a parallel between the precursors of World War II as formulated by the philosopher/psychoanalyst Roger Money-Kyrle (1988–1980) and the Russian war against the Ukraine. The first phase of Money-Kyrle’s theory on the origin and psychological causes of war, written between the two World Wars, describes how group processes can assemble, split off, project and rearrange a sense of outside “threat.” “National paranoia,” coupled with a claustrophobic fear of encirclement and defence against it, leads to war. Omnipotent elation, characteristic of the manic defence, can be seen in the euphoria of war fever. Normal inhibition by social norms and the individual’s conscience are transformed and distorted by group regression and lead to perversion of the superego. Under such conditions, restitutive functions of normal superego functioning, which would normally prevent the outbreak of war, break down. Mr Weiss noted that Money-Kryle’s “restitution” to normal superego functioning as described in his “Voices of Joan of Arc” (1933) is later called “reparation” by Melanie Klein (1937, 1958). Analysing a propaganda event with Hitler and Goebbels in 1932, Money-Kyrle observed how an undercurrent of primitive, emotional messages can be communicated from a leader to a group through projective identification, becoming a monster without thought, creating a “bolero” of repetitive, martial ecstatic sound, which Francis Grier (2021, 2023) calls the “musicality” of the propaganda. The “Pleistocene monster” of the crowd is invited to feel self-pity, then external and internal enemies who are to blame and be hated are named. A poisonous omnipotence is stirred up, and then the crowd is called upon to unite and even die for the cause. Those susceptible to such “usurpation of the superego by a megalomaniac ego” have borderline psychotic personality traits, including a distortion of inner and outer reality due to projection, splitting and manic omnipotence, which are more toxic, manipulative, and dangerous than what is

A Multicentre Study: Comparison of 3-LM Group Output and Therapeutic Outcome Measures. Adding to the Trustworthiness of the 3-LM? Part 2

Andrea Quiroga , Saskia Aufenacker , Alan Crawley , Sherwood Waldron , Laura Borensztein , Camila Botero , Santiago Juan and Laura Bongiardino

Laura Bongiardino

Buenos Aires, Argentina
andrearodriguezquiroga@gmail.com
Via Augusta 64, Atico 1, Barcelona, Barcelona, 08006, Spain

Buenos Aires, Argentina
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0253-691X

Buenos Aires, Argentina

Buenos Aires, Argentina

New York, New York state, USA

Buenos Aires, Argentina

Buenos Aires, Argentina

Buenos Aires, Argentina

Buenos Aires, Argentina

Laura Bongiardino  

3-LM, trustworthiness, process and outcome measures, working parties, training

This is the second report (part 2) of an investigation whose general objective was to provide evidence regarding the trustworthiness of the Three-Level Model (3-LM). Three groups of analysts from different IPA regions worked with this model on the same clinical case. SPECIFIC OBJECTIVES: 1) To analyze group output with the same structured qualitative methodology (part 1); 2) To compare the results of the 3-LM group output reported in the 3-LM forms with the process-outcome measures used in the clinical case (part 2); 3) To describe the presence-absence of anchor points in the groups, their similarities and differences (part 2); 4) To exemplify the interrelationship of the three levels of the 3-LM in relation to patient change and the analyst's interventions (part 2). Sample: verbatim transcript of three 3-LM groups. Materials: 3-LM forms, process and outcome measures (APS, DIS and PHI-RADIO) and Operationalized Psychodynamic Diagnosis. RESULTS: Objective 2 found global convergence in the PHI, DIS and APS scores across sessions. Objective 3 showed convergences and divergences in the themes relating to the anchor points. Objective 4 showed how an anchor point corresponds to the focus of treatment and how this is present in the analyst's attitudes and interventions. DISCUSSION: The results offer preliminary support for the trustworthiness of the 3-LM.

responseSet.title: Response to Igor Romanov
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Igor Romanov

David Bell

David Bell

davidlbell@hotmail.com

David Bell  

I thank the editors for offering me the possibility of responding to Igor Romanov’s letter.

However, before I address the issues specifically I would like to firstly state the following. It is now nearly a year since the Russians invaded Ukraine and almost daily we have news of the growing catastrophe, the reports of the carnage of war, the relentless bombing of civilians. I think we might be in danger of becoming fatigued by these images and we need to retain our capacity to be shocked. I am deeply aware that I write this letter from my secure, comfortable world with only a distant appreciation of what Igor, his friends, family and colleagues face.

In my article I point out that seeking to understand some of the factors involved in any socio-political event must be distinguished from justifying it. In my view, Romanov does not grasp this fundamental analytic distinction. Perhaps an analogy will help: there is a huge wealth of literature that has sought to deepen our appreciation of the historical, economic, and cultural forces, which both created the soil for Nazism and, because it served their interests, colluded with its growth. One might cite the aristocracy, catholic church, haute bourgeoise and various financial interests. Pursuing this broader understanding would, I think, not easily be misunderstood as exonerating Hitler (by using this example as an illustration, I do not mean to imply here that there is any particularly useful comparison to be made between Putin and Hitler).

Thus, it is entirely appropriate, indeed I would argue vital, that we engage in this broader understanding at our current historical juncture. As psychoanalysts we should be wary of the simple binaries which so easily serve to paint ourselves (the so called ‘West’) as excessively virtuous and so obscure any responsibility for the current situation.

As I suggest in my paper, there is a wide tranche of opinion, not only on the Left, that views the war in Ukraine as related to geopolitical processes which have been in motion for some considerable time, particularly since the collapse of the Soviet Union.

responseSet.title: The Deleterious Impact of Idealization and Fanaticism and Their Underlying Causes in Psychoanalytic Education
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Deleterious Impact of Idealization and Fanaticism and Their Underlying Causes in Psychoanalytic Education

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

990 Highland Drive, Suite 104A, 92075, Solana Beach, CA, USA
sugarmanalan@gmail.com

Alan Sugarman  

Idealization, fanaticism, narcissism, superego, psychoanalytic education

Acrimony, dissension, and controversy have characterized psychoanalytic education from the beginning. The usual scapegoat for this situation has been the training analyst system. But this system is seen as just a symptom of deeper, underlying conflicts about our field and preparing for it. In particular, the tendency toward authoritarian dogmatism amongst psychoanalysts is a prime contributor. This inclination derives from the defensive idealization and fanaticism characterizing many psychoanalysts. Such idealization helps us to manage the many uncertainties that characterize our theory, our practice, and our pedagogical efforts. Various methods to address these uncertainties more directly are suggested as a way to counteract the negative effects of fanaticism.

responseSet.title: Letter from Cartagena
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Cartagena

Martha Isabel Jordán-Quintero

Martha Isabel Jordán-Quintero

marthaijordanq@gmail.com

Martha Isabel Jordán-Quintero  

Warm greetings and a heartfelt welcome to Cartagena de Indias, Colombia, on behalf of the three psychoanalytic component societies of the IPA. We have high expectations, both academically and socially. The 53rd IPA Congress, “Mind in the Line of Fire”, brings us together as we celebrate the opportunity to reunite in person once again.

History

Ours is a nation renowned for its diversity and richness in various aspects, including its culture, biodiversity, landscapes and ethnic variety. We are the result of a rich cultural heritage, which stems from the fusion of indigenous, European, and African influences that are evident in phenotypes, skin colours, architectural styles, musical rhythms, dance instruments, food, beverages, religious practices, festivities, traditions …

Colombia is one of the most biodiverse countries globally. It has a unique geographical location in the northwestern corner of South America, featuring both Pacific and Caribbean coastlines. After arriving from Ecuador, the Andes Mountain range divides into three branches separated by fertile valleys irrigated by the Magdalena and Cauca rivers. The geographical complexity continues with the Chocó biogeographic region in the northwestern area, the Eastern prairies and a significant portion of the Amazon Rainforest towards the south.

Our country offers a stunning variety of landscapes. The resulting diverse ecosystems and varied climate zones contribute to its rich flora and fauna, including many endemic species. Last month we were recognized once again as the number one country for bird species globally. Colombia is known for its ethnic diversity. Its mainly mestizo population is the result of the blending of indigenous people with those of African and European ancestries. The ethnic mosaic of the nation has been enriched with migrations coming from the Middle East and Asia. Colombia’s culinary scene is as diverse as its population.

On the dark side, Colombia has faced complex and multifaceted violence over many decades that initially affected mostly the rural areas and progressively the country as a whole. The conflict has involved various actors, including guerrilla groups, paramilitary organizations, drug cartels and state forces. The violence has resulted in numerous human rights violations, including forced displacement, disappearances, kidnapping and extrajudicial killings. Poverty, social inequality, resulting trust issues and transgenerational trauma are ubiquitous, negatively impacting the social fabric. Fortunately, efforts to negotiate peace agreements and demobilize armed groups in recent years have been commendable - they have largely been successful in decreasing levels of violence. The pain became visible, measures have been established leading to the recognition of the horrors caused and suffered, and forgiveness has been demanded - and granted - giving rise to paths of reconciliation and reparation.

responseSet.title: How Psychoanalysis Can Contribute to Understanding Racism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Psychoanalysis Can Contribute to Understanding Racism

Sharon Numa

Sharon Numa

London, UK
sharonnuma@gmail.com

Sharon Numa  

“Not everything that is faced can be changed. But nothing can be changed until it is faced.” James Baldwin (New York Times 1962)

In this article I hope to highlight what psychoanalysis has to offer to an understanding of racism and to point to its potential to contribute further. Psychoanalysis has an impressive reach in relation to its understanding of mental phenomena, and particularly of pathological states, investigating at a deep level the unconscious drives, motivations and phantasies that shape our inner world and colour our emotional experiences. It was one of Freud’s significant contributions to demonstrate that the ego is not master in its own house. We are not just creatures of reason, being in fact propelled by all sorts of unconscious thoughts and memories. These unconscious forces may also limit our development – compromising the stability of our sense of self, our identity, and restricting our ability to engage not only with ourselves but with our objects and our external environment. Even when we are able to achieve greater emotional maturity (which we could think of in terms of the depressive position described by Melanie Klein), in the face of certain deprivations, frustrations or other unwelcome affects arising from within, we are prone to retreat to a paranoid-schizoid world of splitting and projection. This is a world where terrifying objects are created in phantasy and through projective identification, in which symbolic functioning gives way to concrete thinking (a marked feature of the racist state of mind). In serious pathology, defensive systems and pathological organisations are erected, a fortress against knowledge of reality and associated anxiety. Whether we think of deep-seated racism as a manifestation of Freud’s “death drive” or as part of the deeply embedded early anxieties that belong to the paranoid schizoid position, it surely reflects the primitive hatred and aggression that need to be expelled outwards into the object world, the “not-me” world. All of these aspects of unconscious life, developed within psychoanalytic theory, lend depth to an understanding of racism at a psychological level (perhaps expanding political and socio-economic levels of explanation that also influence and are influenced by group behaviour). As Freud noted in Civilisation and Its Discontents: “It is always possible to bind together a considerable number of people in love, so long as there are other people left over to receive the manifestations of their aggressiveness” (Freud 1930, 114).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Terrorism: The Impact on the Fabric of Our Communities and in Our Consulting Rooms

Johanna Velt

Johanna Velt

veltjohanna1@gmail.com

Johanna Velt  

Chairs: Randi Wirth, Maureen Murphy

Moderator: Timothy Keogh

Discussants: Jeanne Wolff-Bernstein, Louis Brunet and Camilo Andres Jaramito

This panel, which took place in Cartagena on Friday 28 July during the International Psychoanaltic Association (IPA) Congress, plunged us right into the heart of the theme and the host country with the poignant testimony of the only survivor of the Oporto massacre, Camilo Andres Jaramito. This is not Camilo’s first attempt at the subject: for over 30 years he has been recounting, over and over again, on various occasions, the terrible attack that almost left him for dead and which cost the lives of five of his friends and at least 18 other innocent victims (the exact number remains unknown). For him, testifying is necessary in order to validate his experience and make it known to the community, a task that cannot be taken for granted, given the prevailing denial about this attack. On 23 June 1990, as Camilo and his friends, young students, are having a drink in the Oporto bar, near Medellin, at around 10 pm, a squad of about 20 hooded men armed with rifles burst in and separate the men and women in the bar. The men are taken to the car park and told to lie face down. Camillo closes his eyes; it’s certain he’s going to be shot. The massacre begins – the worst mass shooting ever to take place in Colombia – the shots rain down, Camillo is shot 9 times: with each bullet, he thinks it’s the “right one”, the one that will kill him, but no, he survives. Silence, he keeps quiet, “plays dead”, he receives a tenth bullet, still without flinching. His friends are dead and the terrorists leave the scene. The women who had been kept at a distance arrive and help him. An ambulance quickly takes him to hospital, where he stays for just a few days, his vital organs having been miraculously spared. “I lost all sense of security”, says Camillo. He quits his university and spends a few years in the United States, where he feels safer … until 11 September, when the tragic attacks echo the one in Oporto. Camillo first studies graphic design before going on to become a funeral director. For 32 years, not a day goes by that he doesn’t replay the film of the massacre over and over again. “Why did it happen? Why did I survive? Why did my friends die?” Inwardly, I associate the same questioning with Primo Lévi,1 who thought he owed his survival to chance alone. He wasn’t stronger, more robust or more intelligent, it was just a question of luck … Camillo feels supported only by his parents during this

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Playing and becoming in psychoanalysis, Steven H. Cooper, Routledge, Abingdon, Oxon and New York, NY, 2022, 192pp, £29.99 (paperback edition), ISBN: 9781032207551 (pbk).

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke

45 Grosvenor Avenue, London SW14 8BU
sgroarke2@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3203-3494

Steven Groarke  

Do we need a concept of play as a discrete event in psychoanalysis? Steven Cooper believes we do. And in Playing and Becoming in Psychoanalysis (2022), he repeatedly cites a small group of analysts in support of his central claim that a greater capacity to play is what best describes a good outcome, or what is attempted at least, in psychoanalytic treatment. Cooper states simply “play is the thing in analytic work” (126). However, I was not necessarily convinced by the book that this is the case. Yet, I nonetheless found the chapters invariably engaging.

The book comprises several (mostly) previously published papers which consider “essential questions about psychoanalysis” (7) from the point of view of play, including the following: transference–countertransference; the setting; enactment; interpretation; fantasy; illusion; dreaming; symbolization; object use; absence; aliveness; mourning; and ethics. Most importantly, the clinical aspect remains to the fore throughout. I found this the most impressive feature of the book – namely, the extent to which Cooper’s focus on the importance of play to the psychoanalytic process is supported, in most chapters, by illustrative cases.

Furthermore, the case material consistently underlines Cooper’s emphasis on interaction and relational contact, although as already mentioned, I was not always convinced that the case vignettes illustrated the phenomenon of play. For me this was particularly evident in the chapter on “the paradox of play in mourning”. Despite the estimable description of Rachael’s grieving the loss of her fantasy of an unavailable father, or Julie’s mourning her melancholic identification with her parents – neither case offered me a better understanding of how “playing serves to help patients move toward grieving internal objects” (99). I think the collection would benefit from a clear and consistent definition of play.

975

[[RunningHead]]

On Brutal Gestures: Trauma, Destruction, and Forms of Mental Illness

Nelson Ernesto Junior , Eugênio Dal Molin and Renata Cromberg

Renata Cromberg

São Paulo, Brazil
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-0707-7356

Rua Oscar Freire, 1513/92, CEP 05409-010, São Paulo, Brazil
eecdm@yahoo.com.br
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-7360-0969

São Paulo, Brazil

Renata Cromberg  

Helplessness, trauma, depressive state due to passivation, André Green, death drive

The Bohemian writer Milan Kundera narrates, more than once, an experience from his years of life under an authoritarian regime. It is the memory of a violent fantasy of rape, one in which libido and destruction are mingled. Based on this memory and how he wrote about it, we present two forms of mental illnesses (by activation and by passivation) and relate them to the model proposed by Green to think about depressive states through passivation. The first form of mental illness, by activation, is the result of an overly successful active defense against anxiety. The second form, by passivation, is a paradoxical reaction to agony in the face of deadly psychic states. Arguing that this second form of mental illness is frequently identified in individuals during periods of political change, we consider that the intricacy between the drives of destruction and the libido, even when it generates fantasies or brutal gestures, can reveal itself as an episodic attempt of an active defense amid the predominance of passivation generated by post-traumatic helplessness.

Psychoanalysis, the Body, and the Oedipal Plot: A Critical Re-Imaging of the Body in Psychoanalysis. 1st Edition. by Fernanda Magallanes, Oxon and New York, Routledge, 2019, 126 pp., $46.95 (hardback), ISBN 9781138391260

Juliet Mitchell

Juliet Mitchell

jcwm2@cam.ac.uk

Juliet Mitchell  

When I first skim-read Psychoanalysis, The Body, and the Oedipal Plot, I panicked. Panic is not my usual reaction when faced with the task of a brief review. Was I not going to be able to understand a word, as so much seemed careless or plain wrong? However, quite differently, was it her fulsome tribute to Judith Butler at start and finish that caused this strange anxiety? There is much in Magallanes book that overlaps with my own work, yet it was as though Butler’s and my reputed hostility to each other’s ideas has created me here as a “cut out”, utterly absent, non-existent; although sometimes I feel I am being quoted, there is simply not a single reference to me.

And I find comfort in a companion – Julia Kristeva, psychoanalyst originator and theorist of “abjection”, who receives just one single mention in the final pages of the Conclusion, where we learn that:

One part of this book uses Kristeva’s concept of abjection in Powers of Horror (1982) and Judith Butler’s notion of abject bodies in Bodies that Matter (1993) as a main point. Although I only mention it once, this idea is central to my thesis. (105)

Indeed, it is. However, more extensively Magallanes and I share psychoanalysis and feminism. I read the Introduction again, this time carefully; it concludes: “It would give me great pleasure if you as readers take this theory to your particular contexts and help me to break, transform and deconstruct it” (3). I calmed down; I liked her. Perhaps, I could take Matisse’s so-positive “cut-outs” to heart.

Maybe in this digital age with social media –Twitter, Facebook and the like – being assimilated rather than acknowledged simply does not matter. After all, in a earlier time this is what happened to the crucial concept of “bisexuality”, and here, that theme appears once again in another guise. Magallanes is much impressed by the work of Preciado on the body as an archive. Preciado (Beatrix and/or Paul) also argues that “sexual difference” is unitary and is not only not binary, but not multiple either. This book introduced me to Preciado and although I have still read or heard only a little, I too am interested and do not share the reaction of the 350 plus Lacanian audience who recently booed them off the Parisian stage. However, perhaps it was not only being a “cut-out” that made me panic – was it that I, myself, was too old to 409

responseSet.title: Untimely Observations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Untimely Observations

Jorge Bruce

Jorge Bruce

jbrucex6@gmail.com

Jorge Bruce  

I am ready to write again. Like a soldier poised to fire, index finger on the trigger. Writing appears before it is begins, like a mission, an antidote, like morphine. What we refer to as style is nothing more than preventing the trigger from going off at the wrong moment.

Ariana Harwicz

Part I

The Spanish philosopher Fernando Savater (1985), who claims he is a mere teacher, has drawn an interesting parallel between the history of cinema and that of psychoanalysis, the trajectories of which go hand in hand, as they were born in the same era. In 1895 the Lumière brothers first showed their invention in public, the same year that Freud and Breuer published their Studies on Hysteria. On reading it, I thought that contemporaneity is not just a parallelism. In both trajectories, a group of adventurers and explorers to the end of the night, or the heart of darkness, gradually ceded to applied specialists, skilled in the use of technique and adhering to the limits set out in the manuals of good cinematographic or analytical practices. This cult of dogma versus episteme bears a familiar resemblance to fanaticism and profitability. Incidentally, family resemblance (“aires the familia”) is the title of Mexican Carlos Monsiváis’ great essay (2002) on Latin American cultures.

The quotation in the first paragraph is not the exception. Rather, it seems to be the fate of humanity's great discoveries. It is therefore essential to combat the stultification intrinsic to both organisations and bodies, which can lead not only to the faculties of listening and looking becoming old, but also, as has been said, to the fanatical walling in and monetisation of time in order to recoup the often considerable investment made. Another paradox of these turbulent times is that the multiple global crises of recent years provide us with an invaluable opportunity to question all of this. We have mentioned the walls, which evokes a plethora of images. Let us consider two of these: borders and barbarians.

responseSet.title: “Enactment of the Mind in the Line of Fire”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Enactment of the Mind in the Line of Fire”

Rogelio Sosnik

Rogelio Sosnik

Manalapan, NJ, USA
rogsosnew@gmail.com

Rogelio Sosnik  

Chair: Jorge Bruce.

Panellists: Jay Greenberg, William Glover, Sergio Lewkowicz, Anna Maria Nicolo and Gabriel Sapisochin.

This was an interesting panel that has an important background. The subject of enactment as a clinical fact became the object of this panel at the International Psychoanalytic Association Congress in Boston (2015), continuing in Buenos Aires (2017), London (2019), Vancouver (2021) and now Cartagena (2023). The sustained interest in the subject and the fact that the panel includes very experienced clinicians made it an interesting offer in our last Congresses. Organizationally, the panel consists of an anonymous clinical presentation that is offered to the audience, and each panellist brings their elaboration on the subject.

The vignette of Rachel, submitted by an analyst who remains anonymous, was read by Dr. Jay Greenberg. In it, the analyst described how in the beginning of the treatment of his patient, a young woman in her middle twenties, who he described as a “very delicate, seeming almost ethereal,” he proposed to her to remain seated during their sessions. After several months, he suggested to her to lie on the couch, without providing any conscious reason on his part, and in the sessions that followed this enactment new material emerged, and provided the basis of the material discussed during the meeting.

After the vignette was read, Dr Sapisochin opened the discussion by affirming that “psychoanalytic listening does not exist without meta-theories that give it a frame of reference.” This was a good beginning that made the experience of the discussion of the clinical material that follows a very interesting one.

The first to present was Dr Sapisochin. I will quote:

In recent decades, a solipsistic model of subjective development had been called into question in different psychoanalytic cultures. The clinical evidence that in every subject there are traumatic elements, as well as the idea that certain functioning of the mind is resistant to change through verbal language, has led to conceptualizations of a model that encompasses early relational history. This model is attributed not to a projective movement originating from the subject, but to the introjection of projective attributions by primordial others, which is always traumatic owing to the cognitive helplessness of the infans. When Freud reintroduced the traumatic theory on the form of compulsion to repeat, the analytic movement gradually began to revisit the trauma theory no longer conceived as an intrapsychic event of economic excess, but a sequela of what was done to one person by another. (J. Greenberg 2019)

responseSet.title: The Ego and the Id: Concepts and Developments
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ego and the Id: Concepts and Developments

Franco De Masi

Franco De Masi

Via Ramazzini 7 Milano 20129 Italia
franco.demasi01@gmail.com

Franco De Masi  

Projective identification, life, death drive

In this note I have limited myself to describing some convergent and divergent developments arising from the innovative concepts present in The Ego and the Id. It could be argued that a part of the psychoanalytic movement wished to emphasize the function of the Ego (Anna Freud, Hartmann, Rapaport), while another part (Melanie Klein and her followers) delved into the dynamics of the Superego and the Id in primitive and pathological states of mind. I will examine three themes presents in The Ego and the Id: the assertion that a part of the Ego is unconscious; the idea that the death drive becomes part of the dynamics of melancholia and its Superego; the concept of fusion and defusion of the life and death instinct. Freud's writing represents a forge of new ideas that have made psychoanalysis ever more creative and capable of understanding the complexity and mysteriousness of the human mind.

responseSet.title: Debating with Colleagues - and with Ourselves
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Debating with Colleagues - and with Ourselves**

Bernard Reith

Bernard Reith

Fribourg, Switzerland
dr.bernard.reith@hin.ch

Bernard Reith  

“We understand our analysands not only with what we know, but with who we are.”1 Our analytic ability to let our internal world resonate, like a sounding board, with that of the patient opens unique therapeutic opportunities but also leads to serious professional hazards, including in our relationships with our profession and with our colleagues.

Identifying with the inner world of our analysands requires going into zones of our own personality which may seem unfamiliar, or which are all too familiar but which we want to avoid because they make us uncomfortable, or which we may keep out of bounds, imagining they are not part of us at all - like cityscapes that include some areas where we feel more or less at home, others that give us the creeps and still others where we will only go at gunpoint. All psychoanalytic psychopathological possibilities can be awakened within us.

Such internal expansions in the analyst, and the defences mobilized against them, are usually unconscious until we become aware of them in the form of moods, dreams or enactments requiring self-analytic attention. We may also discover them through what our analysands unconsciously tell us about our own unconscious: our work of listening to how our patients listen to our interpretations, as described by Haydee Faimberg (1996), can reveal that some object in our inner world has unexpectedly used our voice to speak up. Most importantly for the present discussion, we may be led into our discomfort and out-of-bounds zones by exchanges with colleagues in supervision, inter-vision, clinical case conferences, working party workshops and research settings. The ultimate outcome can be personal growth (which may be why many analysts thank their analysands in their book prefaces), but the process can be deeply disturbing. I will concentrate this essay on the disturbance, examining its impact on our practice and theory, and thence on our ability to debate about practice and theory with colleagues.

We do difficult work, often with patients who suffer severely. We are regularly confronted with the limits of what we can offer them. Our sadness about not being better equipped to help our analysands can arouse both guilt and shame. Guilt, one of the

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference

Steven Ellman and Lissa Weinstein

Lissa Weinstein

New York, NY, USA
sellman174@aol.com

New York, NY, USA

New York, NY, USA

New York, NY, USA

Lissa Weinstein  

Transference, contemporary and classical views, analytic relationship, object relations

The article presents a view of transference from the early versions of transference by Classical/Structural analysts, through a variety of analytic positions that extend and implicitly question the concept of transference. After looking at Brenner and Bird's transference positions, we trace the beginning of relational analysis as articulated by Gill. The differences among several subsequent relational positions are then explicated. Self-Psychology is conceptualized as providing a clarification of Freud's view of narcissism leading to a new perspective on narcissistic transference states. The article concludes by detailing Contemporary Freudian concepts that integrate various object relation positions within an overriding Freudian theoretical structure. The Contemporary Freudian positions in some ways mirror Ogden's attempts to integrate diverse positions.

responseSet.title: A Response to David Bell’s Paper ‘Psychoanalytic Reflections on the Conditions of Possibility of Human Destructiveness’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Response to David Bell’s Paper ‘Psychoanalytic Reflections on the Conditions of Possibility of Human Destructiveness’

Igor Romanov

Igor Romanov

igromanov@yahoo.com

Igor Romanov  

I greatly appreciate David Bell’s recent article; it is full of good points and typically deeply insightful. I have always found his work interesting, and along with my Ukrainian colleagues, we have especially valued his support for psychoanalysis in Ukraine. David Bell’s paper focused on the investigation of human destructiveness, particularly war. However, I was surprised by one part of David Bell’s reasoning – the search for the causes of the current war in so-called neoliberal politics. He writes:

The criminal assault on Ukraine is unconscionable and unjustified. But recognizing the brutality of the aggressor does not release “the West” from examining its own historical responsibilities. I have in mind the catastrophic socio-economic consequences of the (post-1990) violent entry of the market into a world which had no structures to manage or contain it - described by Will Davies as “‘(the) shock therapy’, which aimed rapidly to privatise and liberalise the Russian economy according to the dominant tenets of American economic thinking, resulted in economic collapse and a fall in life expectancy unprecedented in an industrialised nation-state” (Davies 2022) and, as many would argue, the provocation caused by the expansion of NATO bases along Russia’s borders (Bell, 2022, p.688).

This sort of argumentation is not new for David Bell. For instance, in the note to his 1999 paper he wrote with irony about the collapse of the Soviet regime:

Of course the Soviet system is blamed for its current economic collapse (it has nothing to do with the collapse of the prices of their principal exports, the economic crisis in the far east and certainly nothing to do with the escalation of interest rates on Russian loans – from 20-200 per cent!) (Bell, 1999, p.23).

As someone who’s spent a significant part of my life in the Soviet Union, I can testify that it was not the collapse of the regime but the very existence of the regime that was a tragedy for its citizens. The state of total demoralization, the loss of moral values and orientations, and the absence of all kinds of freedoms characterized the late USSR (Rustin and Romanov 2021) - I think Andrey Balabanov's movie “Cargo-200” conveys this athmosphere in the very precise and impressive way. We may recall that rural residents only started receiving pensions in the 1970s and were only given passports in the 1960s. I won’t even mention the topic of Stalin’s repressions. Is the question of why the Soviet system collapsed – a system that makes people slaves – worth raising? Isn’t it more important to ask why certain people become so attached to systems of this kind, reproducing them, and why certain outside observers are inclined to sympathise with them or even idealise?
