responseSet.title: Falling, Primitive Separation and Encapsulated Body Engrams - Working Through a Bodily Encoded Unconscious Syndrome
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Falling, Primitive Separation and Encapsulated Body Engrams - Working Through a Bodily Encoded Unconscious Syndrome

Sebastian Leikert

Sebastian Leikert

s.leikert@web.de

Sebastian Leikert  

Body, unconscious, separation, trauma, technique of treatment

Besides the symbolic unconscious, psychoanalysis today investigates unconscious structures that are dissociated from mental functioning (Lombardi 2017) and encoded in bodily inscriptions. These bodily configurations often stay outside of the psychoanalytical attention and technique of treatment. Two concepts - encapsulated body engrams and somatic narration - provide a theoretical and technical proposition for the bodily encoded unconscious. Within this frame, the paper focuses on new aspects. It is outlined that the encapsulated body engrams result from a traumatic disorganization of the primal relation to the caregiver leading to an impossibility of a separation from the mother’s body. Separation is now feared as a deadly fall into an endless abyss. However, this element is no longer viewed as an unconscious phantasy that can be interpreted but as the perception of a disorganized bodily syndrome that must be worked through with a considerable reverberation-time (Birksted-Breen 2009) in a body-to-body dialogue. Somatic narration encourages the patient to describe his painful bodily perception and invites the body-self to show up during the analytical encounter. Working this way allows the patients disorganized body-self to slowly develop into a container to harbor, organize and symbolize emotions. Four clinical examples illustrate this manner of working.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dream Interpretation and Empirical Dream Research – An Overview of Research Findings and Their Connections with Psychoanalytic Dream Theories

Christian Roesler

Christian Roesler

Freiburg, Germany
Karlstr. 63, Freiburg 79104
christian.roesler@kh-freiburg.de
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3271-5521

Basel 4055, Switzerland

Christian Roesler  

Dream interpretation, psychoanalytic dream theories, empirical dream research

The paper confronts psychoanalytic dream theories with the findings of empirical dream research. It summarizes the discussion in psychoanalysis around the function of dreams (e.g. as the guardian of sleep), wish-fullfilment or compensation, whether there is a difference between latent and manifest content, etc. In empirical dream research some of these questions have been investigated and the results can provide clarifications for psychoanalytic theorizing. The paper provides an overview of empirical dream research and its findings, as well as of clinical dream research in psychoanalysis, which was mainly conducted in German-speaking countries. The results are used to discuss the major questions in psychoanalytic dream theories and points out some developments in contemporary approaches which have been influenced by these insights. As a conclusion the paper attempts to formulate a revised theory of dreaming and its functions, which combines psychoanalytic thinking with research.

paul.denis54@orange.fr
67 rue de Tocqueville 75017 Paris, France

Paul Denis  

The case of the Controversial Discussions in the British Psychoanalytic Society is an example of a failure that has left two psychoanalytic schools of thought coexisting within the same Society without having elaborated their fundamental differences: the only accepted shared elements, being limited to the frequency and duration of sessions, were not clinico-theoretical. At the meetings of the British Society, many speakers, before intervening, announced their membership in this or that subgroup before announcing: “As a contemporary Freudian, I would say that … ” In many congresses and symposia, “the Controversies” persist in parallel presentations, in which one position is listened to by the other as an erroneous one justifying their own position.

In France, the antinomy between those groups subservient to Lacan and those French societies that belong to the IPA is intense. Behind the theoretical differences lurk accusations of malpractice. But I would wager that such implicit accusations are universal. Serge Lebovici told me that one day, when he was attending a meeting of the British Society, his neighbour, seeing Winnicott entering, said to him: “Here comes the assassin … ” Our attachment to theories that we consider “true” are partly ways to reassure ourselves, to allay feelings of guilt: “My patient is not progressing that well, but I have worked according to the proper procedures.”

The fact that the IPA has accepted a relative theoretical plurality among its constituent societies accentuated what Jorge Canestri and Jacqueline Amati-Mehler have called the “Babel of the Unconscious”. One can only negotiate if one speaks a common language, and we are seeing entrenchments within radical changes in the meaning of terms designating fundamental concepts and in the very conception of treatment. An example of this is the fact that the term “sublimation”, which classically describes a destiny of the sexual drives, which change object and are inhibited as to their aim, takes a whole other meaning in an author who claims allegiance to Bion, in this case Giuseppe Civitarese, where it is no longer a question of the destiny of a drive, but of a spiritual elevation of “emotions” towards “the sublime”. The same term no longer has the same meaning and its new signification goes hand in hand with the disappearance of infantile sexuality as an organizer of the psyche. The ever-widening gulf between certain groups, Bionians for example, and other psychoanalytic societies, suggests that we are facing dissensions as profound as those of Jungianism or Adlerism, or Kohut’s self psychology.

responseSet.title: Invisible-Visual Hallucinations in Bion’s “Attacks on Linking”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Invisible-Visual Hallucinations in Bion’s “Attacks on Linking”*1*1

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1, 27100 Pavia, Italy
gcivitarese@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3466-4329

Giuseppe Civitarese  

Writing Attacks on Linking, it is as if Bion had listened to his former analyst. In a lecture on technique given the year before, Klein expressed the wish that someone would write “a book about linking […] one of the essential points in analysis”. Later taken up and commented on in Second Thoughts, Attacks on Linking, has become perhaps Bion's most famous paper and, Freud aside, the fourth most cited article in the whole of psychoanalytic literature. In the short and scintillating essay Bion presents the enigmatic and fascinating concept of invisible-visual hallucinations, which subsequently seems never to have been taken up and discussed as such by other scholars. The author's proposal is therefore to reread Bion's text starting from this concept. To try to give a definition that is as clear and distinct as possible, a comparison is made with those of negative hallucination (Freud), dream screen (Lewin), and primitive agony (Winnicott). Finally, the hypothesis is formulated that IVH could give us the model of what stays at the origin of any representation; i.e. a micro-traumatic inscription of the trace of stimuli (but which may come to be actually traumatic) in the psychic fabric.

responseSet.title: Like the Belly of a Bird Breathing: On Winnicott’s “Mind and Its Relation to the Psyche-Soma”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Like the Belly of a Bird Breathing: On Winnicott’s “Mind and Its Relation to the Psyche-Soma”

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

San Franicsco, CA, USA
thomas.ogdenmd@gmail.com

Thomas Ogden  

In “Mind and its relation to the psyche-soma,” Winnicott reinvents the concept of psyche-soma by viewing it as a set of experiences located neither in the body nor in the brain, and in fact, not located anywhere. Psyche, in health, is understood to be the imaginative functioning of mental processes, and soma is understood to be the experience of physical realness and aliveness. Winnicott offers a clinical illustration of work with a patient who feels unreal to herself. He describes a juncture in the analysis in which the patient's somatic functioning is everything, while Winnicott, by feeling his own breathing and watching the patient breathe, knows that she is alive. This is the beginning of her becoming able to experience her breathing (soma) and imagining (psyche) as real, alive, and her own.

Among the concepts Winnicott alludes to, and that I develop, are (1) the idea that in his clinical work Winnicott not only lives an experience with the patient, he also brings an unspoken structure of meaning to the experience, and the two are inseparable; and (2) the idea that Winnicott introduces a set of terms and a way of thinking that is independent of the differentiation of conscious and unconscious mind (Freud's topographic model). These ideas include aliveness and deadness, realness and unrealness, being and disruption of being.

responseSet.title: A Kleinian Appreciation of the Ego and the Id (1923–2023)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Kleinian Appreciation of the Ego and the Id (1923–2023)

John Steiner

John Steiner

London, United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
63 Cholmley Gadens, NW6 1AJ, London
johnsteiner28@gmail.com

John Steiner  

Ego, guilt, projective identification

I divide this paper into three parts. First, I discuss Freud’s ideas about repression and the unconscious sense of guilt in order to compare them with Klein’s view that we disown uncomfortable facts through a process of splitting and projection, leading to a paranoid defence against guilt. Second, I describe Klein’s struggle to understand the origin and the severity of the primitive super-ego which was so prominent in her child patients. She considered that an important aim of analysis was to moderate the severity of the super-ego to create a more humane inner world. Finally, I will elaborate on her view, as well as Freud’s, that some of the primitive terrifying objects that lie buried deep in the unconscious are fixed and unmodifiable. I will use some ideas from Ron Britton to suggest that instead of trying to modify or get rid of these persecuting figures, it might be possible to stand up to them and to emancipate the ego from the tyranny of the super-ego.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Continuities, Discontinuities and Transformations in the Psychotherapy of a Psychotic Adolescent During the Time of Coronavirus**

Luca Quagelli

Luca Quagelli

Courbevoie, France
lucaquagelli@hotmail.com

Luca Quagelli  

Body, Setting, Undifferentiated part of the personality, Covid, Remote psychotherapy, Adolescence

The author describes the evolution of the psychotherapy of a psychotic adolescent in the period when the pandemic induced their national authorities to impose lockdown. The difficulty of coming to terms with an ever-present reality that proved to be distressing for both the patient and the analyst, as well as with the violence and rapidity with which the external situation developed, leading to a change in the therapy setting, are at the heart of the reflections in this paper. The “choice” of whether to continue the sessions over the phone determined the emergence of some distinctive issues related to discontinuity and to the impossibility of relying on visual perception. However, to the analyst’s surprise, it also favoured the possibility of working through the meaning of some autistic mental areas which, up to that moment, had never really been accessible to verbalization. Questioning the meaning of these changes, the author develops a broader reflection about the way that, for analysts and patients, modifications in the frames of our daily lives and clinical practice have enabled the deployment of undifferentiated parts of the personality which had previously been secretly deposited in the “body” of the setting and therefore were inaccessible.

[[RunningHead]]

Taking Outside in, Turning Inside Out: Exploring Social Dimensions and Psychotherapeutic Process in the Play Therapy of a Young Girl in Foster Care

Ayelet Barkai , Anonymous , Neil Altman , Carol Rubin and Ruth Drasin

Ruth Drasin

Boston, USA

Newton, Massachusetts, USA
ayelet_barkai@hms.harvard.edu

Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA

Newton, USA

A Home Within, Cambridge, USA

Brookline, USA

New York, USA

Needham, USA

Brookline, USA

Cambridge, USA

Ruth Drasin  

Foster care, complex trauma, attachment

This paper, a collaborative effort, describes the work of A Home Within (AHW), a volunteer community-based organization providing pro-bono long term psychotherapy to current and former foster youth. We provide a brief description of the treatment model, present a report of treatment conducted by an AHW volunteer, and discuss further reflections on the societal context of our psychoanalytically-informed work. In-depth psychotherapeutic process from the treatment of a young girl in a pre-adoptive foster placement elucidates the psychotherapeutic possibilities when a psychoanalytic treatment model is accessible to current and former foster youth, usually deprived of this form of treatment due to overburdened, underfunded community mental health systems in the U.S. Open-ended psychotherapy afforded this traumatized child an unusual opportunity to work through past relational traumas in order to form new and more secure attachment relationships. We reflect further on the case from the vantage points of both the psychotherapeutic process and the greater societal context of this community-based program.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mourning in Hamlet: Turning Ancestral Ghosts into Ancestors

John Steiner

John Steiner

johnsteiner28@gmail.com

John Steiner  

Hamlet, mourning, revenge

Extracts from Shakespeare’s Hamlet are used to show how obstacles to mourning may arise from the persistent demands of melancholic internal objects demanding repair and revenge. It is only with the development of symbolic function as a result of separateness between self and object that reparation becomes possible and ghosts are turned into ancestors.

responseSet.title: Passivity and Gender: Psychical Inertia and Maternal Stillness
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Passivity and Gender: Psychical Inertia and Maternal Stillness

Lisa Baraitser

Lisa Baraitser

London, UK
l.baraitser@bbk.ac.uk

Lisa Baraitser  

Passivity, gender, maternal, femininity

Who is afraid of passivity? Historically, women and minoritized people have had good reason to be, given that passivity has been a way to keep them out of the world of “reason.” Freud’s move from the activity/passivity binary as the principle of all instinct, to its gendering as femininity/passivity and masculinity/activity, leads him to assert the “repudiation of femininity” as the bedrock of psychic life (Freud, S. 1937. “Analysis Terminable and Interminable.” In The Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud, 23, 209–254. London: Hogarth Press). This has led to a generative history of feminist, queer and Black psychoanalytic scholarship that constantly re-opens the question of female subjectivity and sexuality, and what we mean by psychic femininity and masculinity. However, what does remains as “bedrock,” even in this theorizing, is the figure of the mother in the internal world of the infant – supposedly castrated yet all-powerful, and requiring that the infant defend itself against what is stirred up as a result of dependency on her. After reviewing some of the psychoanalytic debates about femininity, I turn to “stillness” rather than passivity to suggest that we can identify a maternal element that is on the side of development, a figuration of psychical inertia that holds the capacity for waiting, stopping, ceasing and withdrawing in a world in which these mental functions are sorely missing.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis of Young Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders. An Adaptation of Technique in the Approach to Three Cases
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis of Young Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders. An Adaptation of Technique in the Approach to Three Cases11

Nahir Bonifacino

Nahir Bonifacino

nahir.bonifacino@gmail.com

Nahir Bonifacino  

Child, intersubjectivity, technique

At present, the therapeutic approach for young children with autism spectrum disorder is a major challenge for child psychoanalysis. These patients place us in a special clinical and metapsychological terrain with its own characteristics and call our technique into question. What tools do we have for working in situations where speech has not yet acquired a communicative value? How can we intervene when an experiential subject has still not been constituted? How can psychoanalysis benefit these patients? The purpose of this paper is to show an adaptation of technique in the treatment of three children who came to my practice between the ages of two and three with a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder. In this context, there were certain elements that in the affective proximity of the transference bond which encouraged the child's openness to representational world and a progressive psychic structuring and subjectivation. These elements were the analyst's active search for an encounter with the child, the non-verbal exchanges between analyst and patient, and the characteristics of the interpretations.

responseSet.title: Debating Well: Why Don’t We, and How Can We?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Debating Well: Why Don’t We, and How Can We?

Elizabeth Allison

Elizabeth Allison

London, UK
e.allison@ucl.ac.uk

Elizabeth Allison  

Methodology, epistemology, transference / countertransference, pluralism

This paper introduces the contributions of Alan Sugarman (US), Rachel Blass (Israel), Paul Denis (France), Luisa Perez (Uruguay), Bernard Reith (Switzerland) and David Tuckett (UK) to a debate on the question of how we can enable productive discussion about our scientific differences and why historically it has been so difficult for the discipline of psychoanalysis to initiate and sustain such discussions. By way of introduction the historical example of the Controversial Discussions in the British Psychoanalytic Society is briefly reviewed and several possible factors contributing to the difficulties are provisionally identified, including over-reliance on authority and tradition, eagerness to supervise on the basis of our own theoretical models rather than trying to understand those of others, the lack of clarity and consensus in our definitions of terms, a lack of mutual curiosity resulting in theoretical silos, an experienced threat to identity when adjustments to our positions are called for, and the lack of a methodology for undesrstanding and / or resolving differences.

responseSet.title: Debating Well and its Obstacles
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Debating Well and its Obstacles

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

London, UK

London, UK

UK

David Tuckett  

In this brief contribution, I revisit ideas that I have expressed over the past twenty years (e.g. Tuckett 1994, 2000, 2001a, 2001b, 2001c, 2005, 2017, 2019, 2022; Tuckett et al. 2020) and collect them together to outline six significant and inter-related barriers currently in place which prevent psychoanalysts, on the whole, from having a productive debate with each other and also with those in other disciplines, or society more generally. There is, however, a way forward, which I will describe in the conclusion. Collectively, these six obstacles have produced a drift into “anything goes” rather than disciplined pluralism. They have also interfered with our ability effectively to deploy the extraordinary insights of psychoanalysis into the world of culture and society to useful and externally valued effect.

The six factors I see as influential are: (1) epistemological confusion; (2) endemic terminological confusion; (3) a psychoanalytic culture of persuasion; (4) the lack of a common observational base; (5) psychoanalytic exceptionalism; and (6) limitations of training. These six factors together create a tendency to basic assumption group rather than work group functioning (Bion 1961) and to arrogance and omnipotence.

In the space available I can only briefly elucidate each of these factors.

Epistemological Confusion

Psychoanalysts start their training with diverse disciplinary backgrounds. They are often ambivalent escapees from their core disciplines - for example, medicine, psychiatry and clinical psychology, and less often social science. They rarely have postgraduate research training of any kind but if they do it is likely to be from a particular disciplinary silo, which almost inevitably means drawing on a single favoured methodology - for example, experimental method, randomized controlled trials, neuropsychology, philosophy, hermeneutics, rhetoric, qualitative interviewing, participant observation, ethnography, etc. Within these disciplinary silos, epistemology tends to be seen in a reduced form, governed by the discipline’s conventions and criteria for publishability.

responseSet.title: Passions of Our Time, by Julia Kristeva. New York: Columbia University Press, 2019. 406 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Passions of Our Time, by Julia Kristeva. New York: Columbia University Press, 2019. 406 pp.

Bjorn Sahlberg

Bjorn Sahlberg

bjorn.sahlberg@icloud.com

Bjorn Sahlberg  

Passions of Our Time is a broad collection, covering most of Kristeva’s writings over the recent decades concerning the topics of psychoanalysis, linguistics, literary criticism, politics, 192 philosophy, gender, sexuality, religion and cultural critique. This book makes it also clear how importantly the themes of humanism, femininity and psychoanalysis intermingle and give profound inspiration to our clinical work as psychoanalysts.

Starting with a moving chapter called “My Alphabet; or, How I Am a Letter”, Kristeva describes her childhood in Bulgaria with the public celebrations of Alphabet Day, in honour of two brothers, Cyril and Methodius, creators of the Slavic alphabet. On their shirts, everyone displays a Cyrillic letter. Kristeva writes:

Imprinted in me, the alphabet overtakes me; alphabet is all around me, yet there is neither all nor alphabet: only a euphoric memory, a call to write, unrelated to any literature … I will never forget this first May 24 when I became a letter. (3-4)

Such a lovely birth of a writer, linguist and psychoanalyst! In her chapter on “How to Speak to Literature with Roland Barthes”, Kristeva writes that she wants to speak not of literature, but to literature. It is not about commenting on the substance and form of the work but of questioning the experience of the writer; of showing its singularity, its enunciation. And when commenting on discourse, she writes, inspired by Barthes, that it is about the very plurality of meaning in the work, which is not about interpreting the symbols, but about their polyvalence; not the meaning of the work but rather the empty meaning that supports them all. Kristeva also says that she speaks of literature not as an “object” but as a subjective experience.

How then as a reviewer, to write to this book, and what are my ideas about the singularity and experience it creates? I would say that Passions of Our Time, from the first page to the last, realizes a personal touch including reliance, passion and love, while at the same time being a challenging intellectual enterprise.

responseSet.title: Trivializing a Traumatic Memory of the Holocaust: The Documentary The Pageant
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trivializing a Traumatic Memory of the Holocaust: The Documentary The Pageant

Bella Habip

Bella Habip

Istanbul, Turkey
bellahabip@gmail.com

Bella Habip  

The analysis of films often helps us to understand more thoroughly particular clinical facts and even revisit some analytic concepts or conceptions. Such is the case for the analysis of the documentary The Pageant directed by Eytan Ipeker, presented to the public in 2020. The film reveals to us a reflection on memory and, in particular, on the trivialization of a memory marked by trauma.

Since the publication in 1895 of “Studies on Hysteria”, written by Freud in collaboration with Breuer (Breuer and Freud [1895] 1955), we know that our memory is not satisfied with storing facts but that it distorts them, sometimes by erasing them, sometimes by modifying them, sometimes by simplifying them or by trivializing their affective content. Starting from the observation that hysterical individuals suffered from reminiscences, from memory without recollection, Freud and Breuer in their approach used talking therapy in order to find the recollections hidden behind the hysterical symptoms. Healing was possible by confronting the patient with their own psychic reality through the remembrance of repressed memories, and especially through the affect found through these memories.

At first these memories were supposed to have a direct origin in reality and the task of the therapist was to make the patient discover them, but this changed later, partly with the discovery of the fantasy world. Nevertheless, the element of reality in therapy was to have a decisive influence on all of Freud’s work as well as on the concepts and practices of his successors. Freud would at first be particularly interested in the functioning of the unconscious with its own laws and defence mechanisms, which are largely unconscious, such as repression or splitting that will be responsible for lost memories while maintaining a link with the historical reality of the patient.

Still following the same ideas, by studying closely the link between the laws of the unconscious and the distortions of memories, it is in “The Interpretation of Dreams” that Freud demonstrates how affects help us to find the psychic reality of the dreamer, and, in this way, the meaning of the dream. The affects felt in the dream, or even the affect absent in the dream, are as valuable as the contents of the dream. Freud emphasizes the fact that the representative contents of the dream do not cause the resulting affect that we would necessarily have expected in conscious thinking. And, surprisingly, the opposite would be just as true in the dream, namely that an intense manifestation of affect occurs when there is content that does not lend itself to it:

[[RunningHead]]

Freud’s Patients – A Book of Lives, English Translation, by Mikkel Borch-Jacobsen. London, Reaktion Books, 2021. 302pp, £20, ISBN: 978-1-78914-455-0

Jayne Hankinson

Jayne Hankinson

j.hankinson1@btinternet.com

Jayne Hankinson  

This highly accessible and readable book chronicles the lives of thirty-eight of Freud’s patients. Using a variety of sources Borch-Jacobsen compiles an evidenced trajectory of their lives, focusing on their relationship with psychoanalysis and in particular with Sigmund Freud.

Throughout, Borch-Jacobsen demonstrates an admirable scholarship, “reconstruct[ing]”, in his own words,

the sometimes comical, most often tragic and always captivating stories of these patients who have long been nameless and faceless. (author’s preface, 7)

His purpose is to “allow the reader to get an idea of Freud’s actual clinical practice, over and above the fabulous narratives he himself drew from it” (8).

397

responseSet.title: The Dynamics of Violence in Everyday Living – an Indian Context
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dynamics of Violence in Everyday Living – an Indian Context

J. Basak

J. Basak

London, UK
basak.jhuma@gmail.com

J. Basak  

A Fierce Climate

On 13 May 2021, all of the Indian newspapers and media broadcast the singular ghastly tale of hundreds of dead bodies floating in the Ganges and the rivers of Bihar and Uttar Pradesh. Governments and states were fraught over the numbers and causes of these dead bodies, resulting from COVID-19, further unfolding the nation’s incapacity to mourn the loss of unaccountable human lives. It was a murderous end, leaving behind innumerable aching hearts and tormented existences of children, parents, friends, lovers who had lost their loved ones, with a total denial of dignity in death to the bodies, as well as an abrasive disregard of the needs of the surviving families and friends in search of peace and closure in their loss. An utterly deplorable success of a system that subjugates human entities, their living and also their death.

Traditional Hindu cremation rites comprise burning the body, but all electric crematoriums were flooded with dead bodies waiting in long queues for over twenty-four hours, while manual burning-wood cremations were being sold on the black market. The community-assigned corpse-burners, usually referred to as “doms” a most unfair, unacceptable subjugation to be confined to the lowest caste in community, were desperately seeking ways of survival against ages of destitution by using this catastrophic moment to their momentary advantage. Often generational poverty may harness such violent survival angst that reinforces the misconception that a little power over money can buy omnipotence and invulnerability against a century-long sociopolitical and economic structural oppression. Such brutal actions on the survival continuum often stimulate internal confusion at the individual level, prompting unconscious disassociative processes and disregarding the adversity of others.

Underlying this survival appropriation lies an inherent apprehension of a constant fragile simmering entity fighting against an unempathic system, fearing a total eradication of one’s being, an internal collapse of a fragile ego structure. When a paralysing communal engagement from birth comprises a violent systemic embodiment of poverty and politics, it often consequentially tends to trivialize every other intersubjective human exchange of passion, commitment, making the body of the human agency the site of all battles for sole survival. This thereby makes every act of striving, no matter how violent, a justified bare necessity for that existence. The rage expressed through such ghastly actions becomes a part of the regular life of the “collective bemoaners”.

CONTACT J. Basak basak.jhuma@gmail.com

responseSet.title: Masculinity and its Discontents: The Male Psyche and the Inherent Tensions of Maturing Manhood, by Michael J. Diamond, London and New York, Routledge, 2021, 138 pp, $27.96, ISBN: 9780367724047.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masculinity and its Discontents: The Male Psyche and the Inherent Tensions of Maturing Manhood, by Michael J. Diamond, London and New York, Routledge, 2021, 138 pp, $27.96, ISBN: 9780367724047.

Matthew Steinfeld

Matthew Steinfeld

matthew.steinfeld@yale.edu

Matthew Steinfeld  

The American psychoanalyst Michael J. Diamond’s book Masculinity and Its Discontents could not have arrived at a more appropriate time. Diamond has been writing about gender and masculinity for much of his career, but his corpus has new resonances in the American present. Prior to the overturning of Roe v. Wade (a landmark 1973 decision of the US Supreme court protecting a woman’s right to bodily and medical autonomy including abortion), one could be forgiven for assuming that social progress would continue to be a linear and upward trajectory, given the advances the US has made to expand the enfranchisement of more and more groups of people over the last century. However, it is these very advances that left many unprepared to appreciate the lengths to which reactionary forces would try to undo them. Rarely have rights granted been withdrawn; progress turned back on itself may be better recognized by another moniker: the return of the repressed. Psychoanalysts know too well that under conditions of stress, regression and repetition of the past are always a possibility, warping our social surround and the perception of time. This recent event has had mind-bending effects, and “time-warped” the US to re-wage the battles of second-wave feminists. Receding from view, at least vis-à-vis American law, are the gains of third-wave feminism, queer theory, transfeminism, theorists of varied masculinities, and an understanding of how the intersections of class, race, gender and sexuality bear on all bodies differently. All of a sudden we seem to be back debating fundamental antagonisms between “men” and “women” that were thought to have been settled 50 years ago.

Ours, of course, is a community that knows intimately that there is no way the past doesn’t have myriad effects in the present, despite political narratives to the contrary. How should we think about the gendered past in present tense? Who are these men (and women) who seem obsessed with the need to curtail women’s rights to bodily autonomy? And more to the point, how should one think about what and who “men” and “women” are?

responseSet.title: Threads of Identity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Threads of Identity

Louise Gyler

Louise Gyler

Sydney, Australia
louise.gyler@bigpond.com

Louise Gyler  

Identity, otherness, passion

Identity is a paradoxical expression for psychoanalysis because importance is placed on 'becoming', the decentring of subjectivity and the avoidance of rigid identifications. There is no settled understanding of metapsychological terms and practices or the relationship between theory and practice. The development of psychoanalysis in Asia creates wonderment and anxiety for both Western and Eastern psychoanalysts. Western psychoanalysts are concerned about the threat to Western ideals and sensibilities to define psychoanalytic identity. Eastern psychoanalysts question whether there can be sufficient space for the conception of Eastern subjectivity within a Western psychoanalysis or it represents a repetition of colonial othering. I suggest that there is a thread of identity that rests on the psychoanalytic attitude and state of mind. A state of mind is associated with Bion's idea of passion and Kristeva's notion of reliance. Chetrit-Vatine has extended these ideas to emphasise the importance of the psychoanalyst's receptive and ethical state of mind.

[[RunningHead]]

On Passivity

Catherine Humble

Catherine Humble

Catherine.Humble@iopa.org.uk

Catherine Humble  

‘… to achieve a passive aim may call for a large amount of activity.’ (Freud, 1933, p. 115)

‘All sexuality begins with the experience of passivity.’ (Laplanche, 1988, p. 160).

In an article published in 1961, the American psychoanalyst Henry Harper Hart argued that the term passivity is in constant use in psychoanalysis, but it has not been adequately studied (Hart 1961). Have we come any further in understanding this commonly used term? The word certainly permeates the psychoanalytic literature, but it’s deployed in such a variety of ways its precise meanings can become clouded. At one moment, “passivity” might denote the infant’s dependency, another moment, the aim of the drive. It is used loosely to describe observable behaviour (he is a passive, accommodating man) without theoretical meaning, and precisely with respect to psychical structures, such as the impact of castration. It can denote ways of relating, as well as cultural disempowerment – the subjugation, the ‘passivation’ of women or minority people. These are all different levels of reality – albeit ones that may intersect. One of the great controversies around the term is Freud’s famous elision of passivity with femininity, giving rise to enduring feminist, psychoanalytic debates. Freud also wrote that the term readily slips into its opposite: that passivity and activity intertwine. If, as Roy Schafer once wrote, the conceptual confusion in the term passivity makes defining it “formidable if not insuperable” (Schafer 1968, p.173), is it worth having a go?

The IJP Education Section has taken up this challenge, as Ursula Orstendorf, Heinz Weiss, and Lisa Baraitser look at passivity from a range of psychoanalytic angles – from a focus on masochism, to a phenomenological reading, to a contemporary reading and reframing of passivity with respect to gender. Before turning to these papers, this introduction will briefly trace some of the historical accounts of passivity in psychoanalytic thought, touching on (the sometimes interlocking) areas of: the instincts and masochism, femininity, infantile passivity, and analytic work.1

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

IPA Congress Cartagena 2023: Asistencia Psicoanalítica en Crisis Humanitarias: En la línea de Fuego (Psychoanalytic Assistence in Humanitarian Crises: In the Line of Fire)

Simone Hazan

Simone Hazan

simhazan@gmail.com

Simone Hazan  

Chair: Ricardo Readi

Presenters: Mónica Cardenal, Jeanne Magagna, Alicia Monserrat

All participants of the panel are members of the International Psychoanalytic Association’s committee Psychoanalytic Assistance in Crises and Emergencies (PACE), Mónica Cardenal being its chair, Ricardo Readi the co-chair for Latin America, and Jeanne Magagna and Alicia Monserrat link members in their respective institutions. PACE is a recently created committee whose importance for the IPA was demonstrated by the assignment of the main auditorium for this event, with simultaneous translation; although there were only about 30 people in the audience, the hybrid modality allowed for an additional 70 people to attend remotely. PACE provides assistance and psychoanalytic interventions around the globe to populations facing crises, establishing links between our field, other professions, governmental agencies and humanitarian organisations.

The first talk, by Alicia Monserrat, was titled “Communication in PACE: ‘Subjectivity Threatened: Context of Social Distress. Tackling by Way of Collective Devices.’” The speaker recalled that the central project of PACE consists of organising work discussion groups with professionals such as teachers, nurses or medical doctors, who attend victims of traumatic situations caused by natural disasters or mankind. Such groups use and transmit psychoanalytic methods to help vulnerable populations, enabling the acknowledgement of unconscious processes which are involved in any task. In this context, the Madrid Psychoanalytic Association offers devices of contention based on the concept of après-coup or Nachträglichkeit, according to which the effect of a traumatic experience can only be thought of through representations in a retroactive way. One of the achievements of these group dynamics is to bring closer to each other what is experienced and what is narrated. The idea is not only to contain anxieties, but also to reflect upon the effects, a project that implies a labour of rescue of the psychoanalyst.

responseSet.title: Effi Briest: The Uncanny, Sexuality, and Trauma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Effi Briest: The Uncanny, Sexuality, and Trauma

Rosemary Claire Davies

Rosemary Claire Davies

London, UK
rosemarycdavies@gmail.com

Rosemary Claire Davies  

Uncanny, sexuality, trauma, après-coup, novel

Here the author draws on Theodor Fontane’s 1895 novel Effi Briest to consider the links between Freud’s paper “The Uncanny” and his elaboration of the trauma of sexuality and the après-coup. Conceptualizing trauma in contemporary clinical theory inevitably draws on the theory of the après-coup: the blow that is inflicted on the psyche when the impact of the early event is retranscribed at a later date. The author considers the trauma that Effi experiences, not the catastrophic trauma of death or assault but the deceptive trauma, is disguised as an unparalleled opportunity for Effi. Her story highlights the trauma of sexuality and the incestuous Oedipal dimension. In the cruel economy of incestuous exchange across the generations Effi, in what she experiences as dreary incarceration, is left isolated with her libidinal yearnings. Arguing that Effi as a seventeen-year-old girl is drawn into the incestuous world of her parents and the mother’s suitor Innstetten, the author describes how the trauma involving the denial of her sexuality leads to her being ostracized and facing psychic death. Captured in the deferred maternal desire, Effi unknowingly becomes part of a system of incestuous exchange. This is a trauma that is only known in the après-coup.

responseSet.title: On Looking into The Ego and the Id 100 Years After its Publication
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Looking into The Ego and the Id 100 Years After its Publication

Howard B. Levine

Howard B. Levine

hblevinemd@gmail.com

Howard B. Levine  

Negative therapeutic reaction, guilt, superego, object relations theory, ego psychology

Freud's publication of The Ego and the Id sparked a diverging set of psychoanalytic models - ego psychology, structural conflict theory, Kleinianism, object relations theories, Lacanianism, etc. - each of which attempted to deal with the clinical limitations of his first topography in regard to unconscious guilt, negative therapeutic reactions and primitive character organizations. This paper attempts to look back on these developments from the perspective of contemporary, post-Freudian psychoanalytic theories.

responseSet.title: “The Ego and the Id”: How and Why Freud Transformed His Model of the Mind
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“The Ego and the Id”: How and Why Freud Transformed His Model of the Mind**

Elizabeth Allison

Elizabeth Allison

London WC1e 7HB, UK
e.allison@ucl.ac.uk

Elizabeth Allison  

Metapsychology, topographical model, structural model

This paper argues that, despite its title, “The Ego and the Id” can be seen as the book of the superego, and although it is a metapsychological work, Freud’s introduction of the new conceptual tools provided by the structural model was a response to the clinical problems he faced. The implications of Freud’s introduction of the superego for the analytic relationship are discussed, with an attempt to deepen our understanding of what he had in mind by reading “Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego” alongside “The Ego and the Id”. Finally, the paper draws on Bion to consider the implications of this remodelling of the analytic scene for listening and interpretation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kairos and Chronos: Clinical-Psychoanalytic Reflections On Time

Bernd Nissen

Bernd Nissen

Limastrasse 9a, 14163 Berlin, Germany
bernd.nissen@gmx.de

Bernd Nissen  

Time, times, present moment

The present paper attempts to define “time” in clinical-psychoanalytic terms. After brief remarks on time, on timelessness, on times, and on Nachträglichkeit, the treatment of a breakdown state is described. The breakdown from the earliest period of the patient's life first manifested itself in an autistoid perversion. It finally occurred in the transference in a presence moment and could become conceivable as a thought for the patient in a turbulent process. Here two time dimensions became apparent: The timeless state of breakdown unfolds in the treatment in such a way that preforms of temporal experiences precede the event of time in presence moment, from which then the times past, future and present can become. In the presence moment and its sublation in the presentational symbol, not only did the breakdown become psychically real, but time, times, and space emerged, albeit dynamically very differently in the analyst and the analysand: for the analyst, past and place emerged with the presentational symbol, while for the patient, temporal location did not occur in the time “past,” but in the place where the perversion was practiced. Past is the place where it happened. For the discovery and use of times, it is necessary for the patient to distinguish the absent object from the retraumatizing one. Then the present absent object becomes the object that was there in the past understanding and will be there in the future. The assurance of this figure of thought is obtained in the use of the object.

responseSet.title: Who Do You Think You are? Some Reflections on Analytic Identity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who Do You Think You are? Some Reflections on Analytic Identity

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK
alemma@icloud.com

Alessandra Lemma  

Analytic identity, ethics, consent

And now what will become of us without

barbarians

Those men were some kind of solution.

C. P. Cavafy (1898)

Let me begin by sharing a personal vignette. It dates back a little over 30 years when I received feedback about why I was unsuccessful in securing a place on a psychoanalytic psychotherapy training. “We thought that you were interested in too many things besides psychoanalysis”, they explained. There may well have been other reasons, but this is what they chose to relay. Needless to say, I was upset. I had very much wanted to train with that particular organisation, but I was also struck by how my interest in other disciplines – research, neuroscience, philosophy – that I had enthusiastically shared at my interview was not considered to be an asset relative to other, evidently more important, criteria in the minds of the interviewers. My interdisciplinary slant appeared to dilute my commitment to psychoanalysis, not strengthen it. My story has a happy ending. I did go on to train elsewhere as a psychoanalytic psychotherapist and subsequently as a psychoanalyst. I am deeply grateful for both these experiences, which inform my attempt to address the question of analytic identity: What is it, does it matter, and if so, why? Specifically, I will approach “analytic identity” as a type of professional identity.

What is a “Professional Identity” for?

Professional identity formation (PIF) is a concept much discussed in other fields, where it is understood to be an integrative developmental process that involves not only the acquisition of knowledge and skills relevant to the work in question, but also, and very importantly, the establishment of core values and moral principles (Wyatt et al. 2021). At its best this developmental process equips us to articulate to ourselves and to others what we do, how we do it and the values that frame our doing of “it”. I want to underline professional identity’s communicative, external-facing function because consideration of “what is a professional identity for?” provides a helpful focus away from the all too familiar pull of the inward-looking internal disagreements we are accustomed to when we approach

CONTACT Alessandra Lemma alemma@icloud.com

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com

Francis Grier  

Once upon a time, analysts simply wrote about their patients. Freud did. Analysts were writing to analysts. In that era, learned journals in many different fields were read by learned specialists, who subscribed to the journal. Numbers were small. It was not expected that the unlearned would read the journals, and it seems they usually didn’t.

In more recent years, this situation has changed in two fundamental ways. Firstly, journals (and other specialist writings) have become wholly accessible to readers who are not specialists. In our field, analysts may write for analysts, but their publications have become completely available to patients and to other interested parties. Secondly, the top-down model between analyst and patient has changed in our field, as it has in all others, so that patients feel entitled to be respected as equal partners in the enterprise of psychoanalysis – and analysts agree that that is ethically correct.

A third development is that in the medical world, the consent of patients to the details of the treatment is now recognised as ethically essential. Psychiatric treatment therefore follows this model. There has always been a relationship between psychiatry and psychoanalysis, sometimes closer, sometimes more distant, often confused and confusing. Freud famously advised against analysts necessarily being medically qualified, and the two most influential analysts of the next generation – Anna Freud and Melanie Klein – were not. Yet the American Psychoanalytic Association continued to insist that candidates must be medically qualified for decades to come. More specifically, the medical model focuses on “informed consent” and is designed to manage issues from participation in trials to agreement to surgical procedures or medication regimens to the use of body parts, etc. “Informed consent” here means being provided with details of possible outcomes for patients (quantified benefits, side effects, complications, likelihood of failure, etc.) in an understandable way.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Exploration of Maternal Representations During a Parenthood-Centred Psychotherapy from Pregnancy to One Year Postpartum

Antònia Llairó , Marta Gomà and Nathalie Nanzer

Nathalie Nanzer

Barcelona, Spain
antonia.llairo@brucsalut.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-4251-665X

Barcelona, Spain
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-4347-7154

Geneva, Switzerland
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4146-0399

Nathalie Nanzer  

Maternal representations play a key role in intrapsychic conflicts relating to accession to parenthood and in the formation of the mother-baby bond. Around the birth of the child, the shadows of past objects are cast on the baby and the parent's self-image. Mother-baby psychoanalytic psychotherapy helps us to understand internal conflicts that tend to interfere with the mother's representations of her child or of herself as a mother, as well as aiming to reduce the risk of difficulties for the child. Through a clinical case, this article explores the development of maternal representations in a course of parenthood-centred psychotherapy that begins during pregnancy and ends 11 months after the baby's birth. The psychic change carried out by the mother frees the mother-baby bond from the projections of the past. Excerpts from clinical sessions illustrate mother-baby interactions during the session, the projection of the mother's internal objects on to the baby, and the elaboration and reintrojection of the mother's internal conflicts. Changes in the mother's representations were measured both qualitatively and quantitatively using the ‘R’ interview, which allows various dimensions of these representations to be measured on a Likert scale.

responseSet.title: Field Theory: The Transference-Countertransference Relationship and Second Look
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Field Theory: The Transference-Countertransference Relationship and Second Look

Beatriz de León de Bernardi

Beatriz de León de Bernardi

Montevideo, Uruguay
deleon.bea@gmail.com

Beatriz de León de Bernardi  

Field theory, transference-countertransference, second look

This paper examines the characteristics and clinical utility of the psychoanalytic field theory proposed by M. and W. Baranger, with particular emphasis on the issues of the transferential-counter transferential relationship and the ‘second look’. The role of central key metaphors embodied in the experience of both patient and analyst is illustrated through the author's personal experience and participation in Three Level Model (3LM) work groups. The risk of moments of intense communication between patient and analyst has the possibility for the analyst to lose distance, making it necessary to go beyond moments of emotional resonance and reverie and to take a “second look” to understand the analyst's involvement in the interactional process in order to prevent the constitution of bastions of the field. The 3LM work groups extend the temporal context to include longer periods of time and a “third group look” that triangulates perspectives through group discussions, allowing for the exploration of transformations that have occurred in the analysis. The field perspective contributes to a deeper understanding of the transference-countertransference relationship. The “second” and the “third look” broadens the analyst's insights and enriches the understanding of the psychoanalytic process and its multifaceted dynamics.

responseSet.title: Illusion, musicality, and evanescence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Illusion, musicality, and evanescence

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

London, UK
fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

Illusion, transference, countertransference

I explore some similarities between experiences of music and of analytic sessions. I focus on qualities of evanescence, the way that music – in contrast to many other arts – in one perspective only lasts as long as it is actually being played. Then it’s over. The analyst-patient discussion in a session is similar. Yet the psychic reverberations of some transient, fugitive moments may last a lifetime. And even when no verbally profound understanding is occurring, nevertheless the patient-analyst encounter is emotionally significant. I illustrate this with a clinical example. I explore transference as illusion, and the relationship between truth and illusion in terms of Bion’s O. I end with thoughts about the paradoxical value of the illusoriness of aesthetics and nature as considered by Freud in his short paper “On Transience” (1916), and the grin of Lewis Carol’s Cheshire Cat, left hanging in the air.

responseSet.title: The Missing: Exploring the Use of Photographs in “Working Through” the Natal Body with Transgender Youth
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Missing: Exploring the Use of Photographs in “Working Through” the Natal Body with Transgender Youth

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK
alemma@mac.com

Alessandra Lemma  

Transgender, natal body, photographs

This paper focuses on how for some young people who identify as transgender, the anticipation, and/or the actual process, of transitioning represents a movement away from something in themselves that feels wrong, painful, or traumatic and that has not yet been consciously recognised as such. This becomes a ‘missing’ part of the self’s experience, locked into the body. I suggest that the process of identifying and restitution of ‘the missing’ part requires working through the natal body in its metaphorical and literal senses, in the service of expanding autonomous choice about how to find a hospitable home in the body. Building on Money-Kyrle’s three ‘facts of life’, I propose a fourth one, namely the inescapable fact of our embodied nature, to underscore that our personal history always includes our embodied history, hence the importance of working through what the natal body unconsciously represents. I describe the use of photographs during psychoanalytic psychotherapy with young people who have commenced social transitioning, to work through visual representations of the natal body in the service of facilitating the working through, in its psychoanalytic sense, of the natal body’s unconscious narrative. I suggest that deploying this visual mode may be especially helpful in engaging young people on the autistic spectrum who nowadays comprise a significant minority of transgender young people.

responseSet.title: Why Analysts Do Not Debate Well and What Can Be Done about It
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Why Analysts Do Not Debate Well and What Can Be Done about It

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

rachelblass@gmail.com

Rachel Blass  

Based in part on my experience as editor of the Controversies section of the IJP in the past years, it is my understanding that when analysts fail to debate well it is tied to three main factors:

Focused debates are founded on more fundamental worldviews: Significant debates of differences between approaches ultimately boil down to different worldviews or visions, and on two difference levels. There are different views of what psychoanalysis is about, what defines its theory, practice and aims; and there are different views regarding the nature of the person and, related to this, what constitutes pathology and cure. For example, the differences that would emerge in a debate between “late Bion” intersubjectivists and Kleinians on whether or not the interpretation of unconscious phantasy is a good thing will ultimately be based on more fundamental differences between them in their views of human nature, emotional and cognitive development, the basic challenges the individual faces in life as well as how one conceives of the task of being an analyst or the meaning of a specifically analytic cure, for example, whether cure is centred on the integration of what is unconsciously known and yet split off to avoid guilt, or on opening the patient to never formulated experience due to environmental deficits. Similarly, debates regarding the relevance of neuroscience to psychoanalysis ultimately may be seen to be debates on different conceptions of the aims of psychoanalysis and of the mind. In this context one may suggest that while many analytic schools consider the analytic process to be one of the gradual unfolding of meaning as it emerges in the interactions between patient and analysts, neuropsychoanalysis adopts a more psychiatric perspective, for example offering patients explanations of their predicaments based on what has been statistically associated with biological correlates of such predicaments.

The fact that what seems like focused debates are actually founded on more fundamental differences regarding basic views of the person and psychoanalysis poses a problem for the possibility of debate of the focused issue. Meaningful debate would require shifting away from the specific issue and turning to the more fundamental ones. Staying on the specific level would result in the discussants talking past each other. (For example, discussants can debate the benefits or limitations of empathy and self-disclosure, but the meanings of these ideas can only be understood within a broader context of their conceptions of the analytic process and its aims.)

responseSet.title: The Visual Image and the Denkbild: Sigmund Freud and Walter Benjamin on History and Remembrance
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Visual Image and the Denkbild: Sigmund Freud and Walter Benjamin on History and Remembrance

Anat Mahalel

Anat Mahalel

Haifa, Israel
anatmahalel@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-2690-9146

Anat Mahalel  

Visual image, Sigmund Freud, Walter Benjamin

The present paper offers a comparative reading of Sigmund Freud’s and Walter Benjamin’s thoughts on remembrance and history. Freud’s dream thought, constructed from visual images, and Benjamin’s dialectical image, and the Denkbild as its literary form, are presented as intriguingly intertwined concepts. They both refer to residues of regressive thought expressed through the medium of the German Bild, which can be translated as image, picture or figure. The visual image (visuelles Bild) and the Denkbild are presented as crucial to the construction of history because they present a dialectic between a condensed experience of the past (beyond the scope of words and representation) and the inevitable transformation of experience into language. Freud’s and Benjamin’s late writings are read in the historical context of European Jewish intellectuals facing the rise of the Nazi regime. The images discussed comparatively here are Freud’s last Moorish king and Benjamin’s angel of history. These condensed images are presented as lamenting figures, images of despair and struggle. They serve as examples of the visual image’s ability to represent the unrepresentable and capture hidden mnemic traces at traumatic times.

responseSet.title: Blank Pain and Pathological Mourning in the Analytic Situation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Blank Pain and Pathological Mourning in the Analytic Situation

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke

sgroarke2@gmail.com
0000-0002-3203-3494

Steven Groarke  

Analytic setting, insincere self, memory, processive intervention, siblings, technique

This paper presents an account of the psychoanalytic treatment of pathological mourning in the context of early psychic trauma. I introduce the concept of blank pain, understood as a negative of early trauma, to describe a distinct type of unthinkable anxiety. And I treat pathological mourning as a defence against the unbearable pain of the latter. Clinical observations reveal the extent to which, in a situation where the patient reacts to environmental failure by maintaining a façade (an insincere self), the construction of meaning depends on the use the patient makes of the analytic process and the setting. Considering these observations, I explore the relationship between the structural phenomenon of blank pain and defensive pathological mourning through the therapeutically mutative action of processive interventions and co-enacted scenarios.

responseSet.title: Winnicott and the (Un)integrated Self
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott and the (Un)integrated Self

Joona Taipale

Joona Taipale

Jyväskylä, Finland
joona.h.taipale@jyu.fi
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1106-7078

Joona Taipale  

Relaxation, integration, unintegration, unit status, holding, impingement

The capacity to relax and letting one's mind wander is one of the cornerstones of psychoanalysis. In cases where this capacity seems hindered, the reasons are characteristically sought from particular and specific inhibitions: what is thereby taken to be interfered is not the capacity of relaxation but only the activation of this capacity in a particular regard. In contrast to this mainstream way of thinking, Winnicott argues that the capacity for mental relaxation is a developmental achievement and presupposes a safe sense of integration. The present article investigates this dynamism. It clarifies how an integral sense of self arises out of primary unintegration, explains how a well-established sense of self grounds the ability to relax, and underlines the centrality of relaxed unintegration in everyday life as well as in the analytic situation.

responseSet.title: Editorial: The World in Conflict
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: The World in Conflict

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

For this year’s cover of the issues of the International Journal, we have selected an image from ancient Greece of the rape, or seduction, of Europa - a lot hinges on this important difference. Zeus was filled with lust by Europa’s beauty, and so, when she was picking flowers with her friends, he turned himself into a white bull and quietly joined the group of girls. Europa was entranced by the bull’s tameness. She started stroking the bull’s flanks, finally mounting and sitting on the bull. Zeus seized his opportunity and galloped off with her, swimming across the sea to the island of Crete. The myth has a happy ending: Europa gives birth to three sons and becomes Queen of Crete.

Psychoanalysts will make what they will of this famous myth, which is open to multiple interpretations. But we chose this particular image because it gives such a vivid depiction of violence, confusion and chaos. The couple - is Europa sexually exhilarated, or tricked and raped, a victim of narcissistic, phallic violence? - are depicted fleeing across the dangerous waters of the sea, over the heads of murderous sea monsters. The myth could be read as a representation of male to female misogynistic violence, and the fact that the myth and the vase date from so long ago (the vase is from about 350 bc), expresses the enduring nature of chaotic, sexual violence.

Such violence seems particularly rampant in human affairs just now with the brutality, cruelty and bloodshed of several wars, including in Ukraine, screeching with unbearable dissonance across the globe. Whole countries can be raped as well as individual humans. And the narcissistic stealing of what is lusted after, followed by post-hoc justification and rationalization (“Look, I’ve made her Queen, she is happy, she has three sons”), constitutes the depressing norm of so much international political life. I wonder if this myth might be useful for psychoanalysts contemplating big-group, national conflicts and controversies. But the myth also looms large in our consulting rooms, in the inner worlds of patient and analyst.

responseSet.title: IJP Open Relaunch
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

IJP Open Relaunch

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

I would like to introduce readers to the new IJP Open.

IJP Open was launched in 2014. At that time, it was very cutting-edge; there was nothing like it in the world of psychoanalysis, and not much that was comparable in other related disciplines. In a nutshell, authors were invited (if they so wished) to upload their submissions to the site, and the psychoanalytic community was invited to comment on the submission. These comments did not have to be full reviews, they could be very short, taking up some particular point, or they could be longer and more associative. They could, of course, be critically appreciative, as well as giving an opinion on possible flaws. After some time, the formal reviews would be posted, which would end the process.

For a couple of years, there was quite a bit of traffic on the site. But then the informal reviews from readers dropped away, and for some years only the submissions and the formal reviews have been posted. In effect, IJP Open has given authors the chance to allow their first submissions to be read by a wider public, without these constituting formal publication, but the main point of the site, which was to give an opportunity for the psychoanalytic community to get involved with the development of the paper, had effectively lapsed.

With the recent institution of the new PEP platform, we had an opportunity to develop the site, and we have decided to go ahead with what amounts to a relaunch and development of IJP Open. The site will now be able to facilitate much more activity than hitherto, particularly in the form of to-and-fros between authors and readers. This constitutes the raison d'être of the exercise, and I hope it will stimulate psychoanalytic creativity, not only of the author in question, but also of the particular group of readers and editors engaging with the paper, and of the wider group of interested readers.

In the years since 2014, transparent peer review has now become standard practice for journals in the sciences, economics, law and some fields of psychology, but IJP Open remains the first psychoanalytic journal of this kind. It offers the exciting opportunity for authors to receive fast feedback from readers (along with the anonymous reviewer reports) and to engage with readers, reviewers and editors in an open discussion. The hope is that this will raise the standards of papers finally accepted in the journal, and foster a lively, interested and critically engaged psychoanalytic community.

responseSet.title: The Enigma of Transference. Freud’s Discovery and its Repercussions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Enigma of Transference. Freud’s Discovery and its Repercussions

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Frankfurt, Germany
weiss@sigmund-freud-institut.de

Heinz Weiss  

Transference, repetition, countertransference, interpretation, intersubjectivity, analytic field

This brief introduction gives an historical outline of the development of the concept of transference in the different psychoanalytic traditions. It goes back to the various meanings of the German term “Übertragung” - transference, transcription, transmission, transposition and assignment - and how they were accentuated by the different psychoanalytic schools. The paper depicts the transition from a mainly intrapsychic understanding of transference as repetition to a more bipersonal and intersubjective approach exploring the different meanings of “intersubjectivity” and the forces that operate within the analytic field. Major developments arose from a new understanding of the role of the analyst’s countertransference and the detection of transference mechanisms in narcissistic, borderline and psychotic states. The exploration of different forms of splitting and projective and introjective identification deepened the understanding of the analytic communication and led to concepts like “acting in”, role-responsiveness, “actualization” and “enactment”. As the author tries to show, all these approaches can find a legitimization in Freud’s original writings, but the main differences concern technical issues, i.e. the interpretation of transference.

responseSet.title: On the Nature of Transference Interpretation and Why Only it Can Bring about Analytic Change
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Nature of Transference Interpretation and Why Only it Can Bring about Analytic Change

Rachel B. Blass

Rachel B. Blass

Jerusalem, Israel
Rachelblass@gmail.com

Rachel B. Blass  

Klein, Freud, transference, transference interpretation, psychic change, analytic cure, psychic truth

Transference interpretation has always been regarded as very important to psychoanalytic practice. However, analysts differ on its centrality relative to other forms of intervention. This paper argues that transference interpretation as introduced by Freud and then taken up and developed by Klein (“transference interpretation proper”) is, in fact, the only form of intervention that could bring about essentially analytic change. To understand why, a taxonomy of different forms of intervention commonly practiced within the analytic situation is presented, including interventions that relate to transference, but do not constitute transference interpretation proper. The latter kind is then described in detail. Next, the paper defines analytic change. It relies on a particular perspective on what it is to come to know psychic truth; one that sees such knowing as a lived state of mind, rather than a state of having knowledge about one's dynamics. This foundational Freudian perspective has been especially advanced through Klein's notion of phantasy. Given this view of analytic change it becomes clear that it can only be brought about through transference interpretation proper. The paper also addresses reasons why it seems especially difficult to embrace this view in contemporary psychoanalytic culture, while stressing how crucial it is to do so.
